x: Module 01 — COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase). Under C19RM, the Global Fund contributed to Nigeria's acute, emergency-phase response to COVID-19: the investment support that helped secure the clinical front line of the pandemic, sustain critical commodities, and maintain core coordination functions as the health system came under stress. Its main focus was case management, clinical operations, and therapeutics in designated isolation and treatment centres that separated infectious patients from routine hospital care, most of which were urban tertiary and second…

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict Strong. Whether the acute-response investment matched Nigeria’s actual pandemic and health-system needs, was designed for the real spread of states, tiers of care and population groups, and arrived in time. The acute investment answered a documented, pre-existing capacity gap and arrived at the height of the emergency. The one real qualification is that primary-care and remote-community reach came late, which is taken up under Equity. 1.1 The investment matched a real, documented gap in emergency-response capacity 1.2 The equipment matched the pandemic’s clinical needs, but reaching lower-level facilities and remote communities came late 1.3 The investment arrived fast despite lockdown and shifted over time toward preparedness

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Coherence: verdict Internal Strong, External Moderate. Internal alignment with Nigeria’s national frameworks and with concurrent Global Fund and government financing, and external complementarity with development partners and the private sector. The response aligned closely with the national frameworks and complemented national and ATM financing rather than duplicating it. External collaboration was effective, but leaned heavily on partners in the earliest phase and brought the pri… 2.1 The response put the priorities of national and global frameworks into practice 2.2 The response complemented national funding and the HIV, TB and malaria grants rather than duplicating them 2.3 Partners covered the gaps and roles stayed clear, though the private sector was brought in late

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Effectiveness: verdict Strong. Whether the intended intermediate outcomes (effective isolation, case management, diagnosis and patient movement) were achieved, the role C19RM plausibly played, the unintended outcomes that arose, and how barriers and enablers compare with the module’s theory of change. Outputs were delivered and the chain from functional capacity to better case management, severity triage, faster diagnosis and safe patient movement holds across both the survey and the interviews. The barriers that arose were of staffing a… 3.1 The funded items were delivered and led to better case management, as intended 3.2 Unintended outcomes were mixed 3.3 Barriers and enablers, tested against the theory of change

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Efficiency: verdict Moderate. Whether delivery was timely and cost-effective, the procurement, sequencing and turnaround factors that shaped it, and how lockdown and the multi-recipient coordination model affected it. The investment achieved rapid mobilisation under exceptionally difficult circumstances, with imported capital assets reaching states during global lockdowns and rapid diagnostic tests breaking a major diagnostic bottleneck. However, deliver… 4.1 Supplies arrived fast under lockdown, but poor timing, over-ordering and slow early testing weakened delivery 4.2 Early waste: weak protocols, and supplies that arrived after they were needed 4.3 The multi-recipient model sped up importing but made coordination harder

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Impact: verdict Moderate. What plausible contribution to case management, transmission control and durable capability can be linked to the acute response, recognising concurrent actors, how it performs against the indicator frameworks, and what population-level effects can be evidenced. A plausible, contributory improvement in case management and transmission control, with two assets that durably outlived the pandemic, and a measurable rise in the Respond-pillar indicators. The most direct population endpoint, mortality, i… 5.1 A credible contribution to better case management and lower transmission, with some lasting assets 5.2 On the national scorecards, Nigeria’s emergency-response capacity improved 5.3 Population-level effects: lower transmission is evidenced; effects on deaths and on HIV, TB and malaria services are not yet shown here

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — OECD-DAC Sustainability: verdict Limited. Whether sustainability was built into design and the transition phase, the extent of institutionalisation and integration into routine functions, and which capabilities are likely to endure and through what mechanisms. Long-term sustainability was not a primary design consideration within an investment intended principally for emergency response. Much of the acute infrastructure was COVID-dedicated and has decayed, and the financing, governance and mainte… 6.1 Long-term sustainability was not a primary design consideration 6.2 Capacity has decayed, and centres recorded as functional are not all actually working 6.3 What is likely to endure, and the missing mechanisms

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — Equity: Free care, state-wide ambulance pick-up and severity triage matched care to need wherever patients presented, but the acute capacity concentrated in urban public facilities and reached the primary-care tier and remote communities late.

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — System risk: Stranded, degraded and vandalised acute assets leave real residual risk, and the deeper risk is architectural: a preparedness capability built largely from COVID-specific assets, without dual-use design or maintenance, decays in the gap bet…

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — Lessons: Anchor preparedness funding to the documented gap: the weakest of Nigeria’s four IHR domains going into COVID was Respond, and the acute investment that targeted exactly those capacities is the model… • Purpose-built emergency infrastructure decays fast once its emergency passes; the assets that endured were the non-specific ones, ambulances, trained staff and coordination models, which is the design… • A binary functional status flag overstates durable capability: 22 of 27 centres flagged functional coexisted with a manager’s 50-to-60% working estimate and a total asset loss, so survey status fields… • Rapid diagnostics delivered the clearest gain, breaking a one-to-two-month sample-turnaround bottleneck, so point-of-care testing is a high-leverage priority for future outbreak response. • Sequencing matters as much as supply: equipment that arrives after training, or commodities that expire undeployed, erode the value of fast procurement and should be planned as a single delivery-and-r… • Embed infection-control and waste protocols from the first day of a response: before the transmission route was understood, Global-Fund-supplied equipment was needlessly incinerated after single use a… • Integrating private and secondary facilities improved reach and reduced stigma but happened late and informally; building it in from the first wave would extend equity and capacity earlier. • Security and maintenance are response capabilities, not afterthoughts: an unguarded high-value asset was lost overnight, and a maintenanc

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — Recommendations: To Federal Ministry of Health and NCDC: Adopt a default dual-use-by-design rule for future acute-response infrastructure, so isolation and treatment capacity is specified to convert to routine or all-hazards use once an emergency passes. • To State Ministries of Health and Hospital Management Boards: Institute funded maintenance and security plans for retained emergency assets, with a named custodian per centre, to prevent the degradation and vandalism documented here. • To Global Fund and the Principal Recipients: Finance a delivery-and-readiness chain (training, equipment, commissioning and commodity-shelf-life management as one sequenced package) rather than separate supply tranches, to close the equipment-after-training and expired-commodity gaps. • To NCDC and State Epidemiology units: Standardise and sustain point-of-care diagnostics with stock monitoring, building on the rapid-test bottleneck-break, and report antigen routine-use and stockout status as a live indicator. • To State Task Forces and Emergency Operations Centres: Embed private and secondary facilities into the coordinated response from the first wave of any future outbreak, formalising the integrated-care model that emerged late but proved durable. • To Federal Ministry of Health and the primary health care agency: Extend acute-response coverage to the primary-care tier and to remote, hard-to-reach communities in future investment, closing the geographic and tier gap this response left. • To Country Coordinating Mechanism, Global Fund

Module 01 COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase) — Investment delivered: Isolation and treatment centres: Designated state isolation and treatment centres for case management; the 2021 fast-track PPE was scoped to 137 treatment centres and 133 PCR laboratories • Advanced ambulances (national PRs): 16, delivered to Oyo, Kano and Jigawa • Lagos State PR window (subnational): Lagos State Ministry of Health, a Principal Recipient in its own right and the country’s epicentre, procured through C19RM: 8 mobile intensive-care ambulances; a real-time PCR machine with reagents and ICT equipment; over 100 hospital beds for isolation and treatment centres; and PPE for health workers • Antigen rapid diagnostic tests: Over 8 million, alongside PCR reagents, extraction kits and laboratory consumables • Personal protective equipment: N95 and surgical masks, gloves, face shields, coveralls and sanitiser, with isolation supplies and triage-area set-up • Critical-care and diagnostic equipment: Patient monitors, infusion pumps, ultrasound machines, and mobile and fixed digital X-ray systems • Therapeutics: Repurposed COVID-19 medicines for clinical management • Coordination and surge capacity: Public-health emergency-operations staff trained: 480 across 12 states • Delivery: Through three Principal Recipients (NACA, NTBLCP, Lagos State Ministry of Health), reaching all 36 states and the FCT

Module 02 — Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics. Medical oxygen is an essential therapy and the first-line treatment for severe hypoxaemia, the dangerously low blood oxygen that accompanies pneumonia, COVID-19, complications of pregnancy and childbirth, trauma and many childhood illnesses. No hospital can deliver critical, surgical, maternal or newborn care safely without a reliable supply. A working oxygen system is a linked value chain: oxygen is produced on site by pressure-swing-adsorption (PSA) plants, or held and moved in bulk as cryogenic liquid oxygen (LO…

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict Strong. Whether the oxygen investment matched Nigeria’s actual need, was designed for the real spread of states, tiers of care and population groups, and evolved appropriately as the context changed. The investment answered an almost absent oxygen ecosystem with precisely the assets the gap required, and went beyond the pre-existing modelled strategy by using real facility data to build surge capacity. The one clear weakness is the tier… 1.1 Before C19RM, Nigeria's oxygen ecosystem was fragmented, inadequate and heavily dependent on commercial supply 1.2 The design reflected differences across states but remained predominantly hospital-based 1.3 The investment evolved from emergency response to long-term oxygen system strengthening

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Coherence: verdict Internal Moderate, External Moderate. Internal alignment with Nigeria’s national oxygen strategy and concurrent financing, and external complementarity with the development partners that co-built the oxygen scale-up. The investment aligned closely with the national oxygen strategy and the broader health-security frameworks, and complemented the concurrent grants by delivering through the ATM Principal Recipients and the national tuberculosis programme.… 2.1 The investment aligned with the national oxygen strategy and the health-security frameworks 2.2 The investment complemented the other grants and government financing 2.3 Partners worked well together and the piping project was a model, but procurement was fragmented

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Effectiveness: verdict Strong. Whether the intended intermediate outcomes (oxygen available, closer to patients, and hypoxaemia identifiable and treatable) were achieved, the role C19RM plausibly played, the unintended outcomes, and the limits of measurement. This is the strongest aspect of the oxygen investment. Oxygen production, storage and bedside delivery expanded substantially; long referral journeys for oxygen were reduced; and pulse oximetry adoption increased and appears to be sustained… 3.1 Oxygen became more available, closer to patients and easier to initiate 3.2 This shift has the potential to make oxygen financing fairer and more affordable for patients. 3.3 Barriers and enablers, tested against the theory of change

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Efficiency: verdict Moderate. Whether delivery was timely, the procurement and transition factors that shaped it, and how the design of procurement and the multi-recipient model affected value. A procurement-heavy programme embedded in the government system ran into repeated delays, the transition between Principal Recipients was difficult for oxygen specifically, and a split between locally procured and equipment-bundled plants l… 4.1 Procurement delays, and delays in completion of BME trainings 4.2 Liquid-oxygen losses and the engineering fixes

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Impact: verdict Strong. What plausible contribution to the health system, preparedness and population health can be linked to the oxygen investment, recognising the concurrent role of government and partners. The system-level contribution is substantial: an oxygen ecosystem and governance that did not exist before, now built, embedded in planning and repurposed for routine care and later outbreaks, and one that informants say no other grant coul… 5.1 A national oxygen system, oxygen on the planning agenda, and assets reused beyond COVID 5.2 Against the frameworks: what is already achieved and what is still expected 5.3 Population-level effects: likely but not yet measured

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — OECD-DAC Sustainability: verdict Limited. Whether sustainability was designed in, the extent of institutionalisation, and which capacities will endure and through what financing, governance and human-resource mechanisms. This is the dimension respondents worry about most. Recurrent power and diesel cost, a scarce and ageing biomedical-engineering workforce, dependence on original suppliers for spares, and single-account financing bureaucracy together threat… 6.1 Recurrent power and diesel are the main running-cost problem 6.2 Financing mechanisms designed but stalled 6.3 Too few engineers and supplier-only spare parts are the main risk to keeping plants running

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — Equity: Selection of PSA plant sites were deliberately equitable, and a PSA plant was earmarked for every state (each senatorial district complimentarily with other non-GF investment streams), with hub-and-spoke distribution built into the design,…

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — System risk: The oxygen ecosystem is real but fragile. Its operation depends on recurrent power, scarce engineers and supplier service agreements, and where any of these fails a plant becomes a stranded asset. Abandoned tanks, unsupported early plants a…

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — Lessons: Oxygen is the portfolio’s clearest acute-to-RSSH pivot: an emergency commodity became a permanent ecosystem with its own governance, the model for converting crisis investment into durable system stre… • Data-driven sizing beat the modelled strategy: counting facilities and building surge capacity proved more relevant than the steady-state plan, so future investment should be led by facility data. • Capital without recurrent financing is fragile: plants that run on expensive power and depend on supplier spares will fail without ring-fenced maintenance funding and service agreements. • Procurement design is a sustainability decision: bundled plants with warranties and preventive maintenance outlived locally procured ones without spares. • Money from capacity that was never delivered is not a saving. Two senatorial districts went without an oxygen plant because insecurity meant no vendor bid, and the maintenance training on a core compo… • The biomedical-engineering workforce is the binding constraint: too few, ageing and migrating engineers threaten the whole asset base, and must be trained and retained alongside the hardware. • Build the data layer alongside the hardware: oxygen and hypoxaemia indicators in the routine information system would let the health benefit of an investment of this scale be tracked and demonstrated… • PHC oxygen was addressed by design through portable concentrators and cylinders distributed to the states rather than through fixed plants, but those portable assets reached the ti

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — Recommendations: To Federal Ministry of Health and State Ministries of Health: Operationalise the approved dedicated-oxygen-account or revolving-fund model in every state, ring-fencing a share of oxygen revenue for maintenance, building on the Akwa Ibom example. • To Federal Ministry of Health, Global Fund and the Principal Recipients: Require service-level agreements and spare-part access with original suppliers for every plant, and standardise on equipment-bundled procurement with warranty and preventive-maintenance cover. • To Federal Ministry of Health, Global Fund and partners: Conduct a dedicated national oxygen-system assessment that establishes current functional capacity, daily oxygen consumption and the remaining need gap, to direct where the next investment and maintenance should go. • To Federal Ministry of Health, Global Fund and Principal Recipients: Complete the outstanding component-maintenance training on the RIX compressor before close-out, and record any undelivered plants or training as gaps to be filled rather than as savings. • To Federal Ministry of Health and State governments: Invest in the biomedical-engineering workforce through training, retention and bonding schemes, so the asset base can be maintained. • To Federal Ministry of Health and the primary health care agency: Extend oxygen and pulse oximetry to the primary-health-care tier and remote communities, the entry point for the poorest, in future investment. • To Federal Ministry of Health and the United for Oxygen partners: Invest

Module 02 Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics — Investment delivered: PSA plants: 73 new plants (10 procured in-country, 63 WAMBO-procured) plus 22 broken-down plants repaired; national platform of up to 73, at least one per state • Liquid-oxygen (LOX) tanks: 12 cryogenic tanks in 12 national teaching hospitals, in all zones except the north central geopolitical zone • Oxygen piping and storage: Wall-piped oxygen to 44 tertiary hospitals and 4,000+ bed points; bulk storage raised roughly tenfold to about 120,000 litres • Oxygen cylinders: 21,291 distributed nationally (reconciled count) through hub-and-spoke • Concentrators: 2,086 ten-litre oxygen concentrators with UPS backup • Pulse oximeters: 7,730 hand-held and table-top units for severity triage • Diagnostics and therapy: 690 oxygen analysers, 42 mobile digital X-ray units, plus regulators, flow meters, humidifiers and ventilation consumables • Systems and governance: Tank and warehouse telemetry, M-supply logistics tools, and state oxygen desks (co-financed with UNITAID)

Module 03 — Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria. Theory-based contribution analysis against the OECD-DAC criteria, with Equity and System Risk applied as cross-cutting lenses. Module 3 assesses the ATM-mitigation investment of the C19RM grant designed to keep Nigeria’s HIV, tuberculosis and malaria programmes running through the pandemic. The conceptual framework for this investment is aligned with the IHR (2005) case-management and response capacities, the national disease strategic plans, the National Action Plan for Health Security, and the Joint External Eval…

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict Strong. Whether the mitigation answered a real threat to HIV, TB and malaria continuity, reflected the differences between the three programmes and the spread of states, and funded what the pandemic actually required. The investment answered an immediate, multi-level threat to disease continuity, and it financed the very adaptations national guidelines had already endorsed but could not resource before the pandemic. 1.1 COVID-19 posed an immediate threat to HIV, TB and malaria service continuity 1.2 The design reflected disease-specific needs but addressed state-system constraints less fully 1.3 The investment accelerated continuity models already endorsed in national policy

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Coherence: verdict Internal Strong, External Moderate. Internal alignment with the disease strategies and the multi-recipient structure, and external complementarity with civil society and the other funders in the same states. The mitigation sat squarely inside the national disease strategies and the two-Principal-Recipient structure fit the task; external coherence is held back by inconsistent inclusion of civil society and parallel partner systems. 2.1 The mitigation aligned with disease strategies and utilised an appropriate delivery structure 2.2 The mitigation fostered integration across HIV, TB and malaria programmes 2.3 Community engagement strengthened delivery, but civil society participation remained uneven

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Effectiveness: verdict Strong. Whether continuity was achieved, the role C19RM plausibly played, and how the three programmes differed. Services were not only protected but in places advanced: tuberculosis notifications held and grew, HIV continuity was maintained through dispensing and digital models, and a first COVID-malaria testing integration was piloted. 3.1 Service continuity was achieved, with the strongest gains observed in tuberculosis 3.2 HIV continuity was sustained through differentiated and digital service delivery models 3.3 COVID-malaria integration emerged as an unintended but durable innovation 3.4 Malaria programme activities delivered despite lock down

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Efficiency: verdict Moderate. Whether delivery was timely and cost-aware, and how lockdown logistics and grant timing shaped value. Lockdown restrictions, delayed grant activation and global supply-chain disruptions constrained the timeliness of implementation and contributed to commodity expiries. However, where delivery structures were already embedded within programm… 4.1 Lockdown disruptions and delayed funding constrained timely implementation 4.2 Embedded implementation structures supported efficient delivery

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Impact: verdict Strong. What contribution to disease-programme performance and continuity can be linked to the investment, recognising the concurrent role of government and other funders. The contribution is strong and, for tuberculosis, partly quantified, and the lasting integration of the three programmes is a durable, partly unintended gain. 5.1 The mitigation protected programme continuity and contributed to improved tuberculosis outcomes 5.2 Cross-programme integration emerged as a durable impact of the mitigation response 5.3 Population-level effects remain plausible but incompletely measured

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — OECD-DAC Sustainability: verdict Limited. Whether the gains will endure, the strength of government ownership, and what most threatens continuity now. Sustainability is fragile and donor-driven: the US stop-work order and Global Fund tapering threaten hard-won gains, with no government takeover yet secured. 6.1 Donor withdrawal poses the greatest threat to sustainability 6.2 Government ownership is emerging, but financing gaps remain

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — Equity: Service adaptations extended reach to patients and communities, but lockdown enforcement inflicted serious rights violations against key and vulnerable population.Despite this the C19RM ATM mitigation pillar made substantial contributions t…
Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — System risk: Residual risk is acute and financing-driven, compounded by stockouts, key-population exposure and workforce attrition.

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — Lessons: Endorsed-but-unfunded models scale fastest in a crisis: the pandemic activated multi-month dispensing, differentiated delivery and community models that policy already backed. • Crisis-response investments can generate programme growth, as evidenced by increased TB case detection and coverage following investments in molecular diagnostics and community outreach. • Digital solutions improve programme continuity and accountability-ICT4D platforms, virtual coordination mechanisms, and real-time monitoring systems enabled effective programme implementation despite… • Integration is the durable dividend: the three programmes emerged more integrated than they entered, and the COVID-malaria model outlasted the emergency. • Embedded delivery is efficient delivery: where implementers were embedded, burn rates and documentation were strong, so the inefficiency was logistics and timing, not competence. • Lockdown logistics, not demand, was the main constraint: commodities expired because they could not move, a planning lesson for the next emergency. • Emergency enforcement can break adherence for key populations: rights protections must be built into pandemic protocols, not added afterwards. • Donor concentration is the central sustainability risk: the stop-work order and tapering expose how much of the ATM response rests on external financing. • Grant timing matters: about ten months of lost grant life meant much HIV funding arrived after the acute waves had passed.

Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — Recommendations: To Federal Ministry of Health, NACA and State governments: Secure a government-financed takeover of the HIV and tuberculosis continuity gains threatened by donor withdrawal, with a costed transition before tapering bites. • To Federal Ministry of Health and the disease programmes: Institutionalise the cross-programme integration, the COVID-malaria model and integrated ATM delivery, as standing policy rather than an emergency artefact. • To NACA, NASCP and civil-society networks: Build key-population rights protections and continuity of adherence support into emergency and lockdown protocols, so enforcement does not break care. • To Global Fund, NMEP, NACA and the Principal Recipients: Fix emergency logistics, pre-positioning and movement-clearance arrangements, to prevent the lockdown-driven expiries seen this time. • To Federal Ministry of Health and State governments: Resolve anti-tuberculosis drug stockouts and non-functional equipment, and secure the recurrent reagents and diagnostic-machine power the molecular platforms need. • To NACA, the Principal Recipients and civil society: Make civil-society inclusion in technical working groups consistent and carry community organisations into planning from the start. • To Global Fund and Federal Ministry of Health: Align grant timing so emergency funding is released within the window of need, given the grant-life lost this cycle.
Module 03 Mitigation of C19RM Effects on HIV, TB and Malaria — Investment delivered: Tuberculosis (IHVN): US$36.5m • HIV (FHI 360): US$5.8m • Malaria (NMEP): US$14.2m • Total envelope: US$56.5m

Module 04 — Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics. Classical epidemiology and emerging insights into outbreak response and management, as embedded in the International Health Regulations (IHR), are underpinned by a country's capability to detect threats. The critical link between surveillance and clinical management during an outbreak is bridged by diagnostics. Laboratory systems are a critical first line for confirmation and pathogen characterisation during outbreaks. It is where a suspected case becomes confirmed, where a pathogen is identified, characterized, an…

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict . Whether the investment was the right thing to do: whether it addressed the most important diagnostic and laboratory-system gaps, and was aligned to Nigeria’s priorities and the realities at national and subnational level. 1.1 Nigeria entered the pandemic with highly centralised molecular diagnostic capacity 1.2 The investment matched the diagnostic gap, with stronger geographic than tier-level differentiation 1.3 The portfolio evolved from emergency testing to laboratory systems strengthening, but implementation timing lagged

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Internal Coherence: verdict . How well the laboratory investment fits with the rest of Nigeria’s own response: with national laboratory plans and structures, the RSSH grant, and the other C19RM pillars, rather than working at cross purposes. 2.1 The investment was implemented through national laboratory structures 2.2 C19RM built on government and RSSH laboratory investments

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC External Coherence: verdict . How well the investment complemented other actors and donors (USG, CDC and PEPFAR, World Bank, UNICEF, WHO and implementing partners), rather than duplicating or competing with what others were already doing. 3.1 Partners built on an existing molecular network with largely complementary roles 3.2 External coherence remained vulnerable to reagent and maintenance dependencies

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Effectiveness: verdict . The extent to which the investment achieved its intended laboratory objectives: building testing capacity, training the workforce, cutting turnaround time, raising quality and biosafety, and making a real difference to diagnosis and outbreak response, with what worked and what did not. 4.1 Activated laboratories expanded local testing and reduced turnaround time 4.2 Workforce, quality-management and biosafety gains were consistently reported 4.3 Deployment did not always translate into operational use

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Efficiency: verdict . Whether results were delivered economically and on time: how well money, equipment, commodities, training, and effort were converted into working diagnostic capacity and where avoidable waste, delay, or sequencing failure occurred. Formal cost-effectiveness is not claimed without verified expenditur… 5.1 Efficiency losses arose from sequencing failures rather than investment choice 5.2 Delayed fund release and push-based supply created avoidable waste 5.3 Ready sites converted inputs into working capacity more efficiently

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Impact: verdict . The higher order changes the investment contributed to, read through the contribution lens and separated into realised, directly evidenced contributions and prospective contributions that depend on sustained operation. Concurrent actors, government, earlier HIV and PEPFAR investment, and concurrent… 6.1 Realised contribution: diagnostic access shifted closer to patients and turnaround time fell 6.2 Realised contribution: specimen referral strengthened the diagnostic chain to LGA level 6.3 Realised contribution: COVID-19 assets were repurposed for multi-disease diagnostics 6.4 Realised contribution: workforce, quality systems and biosafety practices strengthened routine laboratory capacity

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — OECD-DAC Sustainability: verdict . Whether the laboratory gains will last after C19RM funding ends: whether financing, reagent supply, equipment maintenance, the trained workforce and government ownership are in place to keep diagnostic capacity running. 7.1 Capabilities are being institutionalised, but financing and maintenance arrangements remain weak 7.2 Reagent supply and maintenance remain the main sustainability risks 7.3 Advanced diagnostics offer major potential but require substantial recurrent financing 7.4 Workforce retention and domestic financing remain unresolved
Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — Equity:

Module 04 Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics — Recommendations: To 1: Develop and operationalise a National Laboratory Reagent and Consumables Supply-Security Framework covering GeneXpert, Truenat, PCR, and sequencing platforms, defining domestic financing, minimum stock levels, forecasting, emergency procurement, and accountability across national, state, and private levels. • To 2: Operationalise Diagnostic Network Optimisation (DNO) findings to guide placement, utilisation, and integration across diseases and tiers. • To 3: Establish a laboratory investment planning and coordination framework requiring end-user and state participation across planning, procurement, deployment, installation, and commissioning, with all investment linked to verified site readiness. • To 4: Complete the national and six zonal biomedical engineering hubs and develop a costed national equipment maintenance and replacement plan for all supported assets, with preventive schedules, spares procurement, and domestic financing. • To 5: Develop a National Genomic Surveillance and Advanced Diagnostics Sustainability Plan that secures recurrent financing for the national multi-omics centre, runs it as a ring-fenced public-private core facility generating revenue from sequencing, local primer synthesis and disease-programme support, and settles outstanding maintenance and extended-warranty payments promptly, integrated into NAPHS and GC8. • To 6: Implement a workforce-retention scheme for specialised diagnostic and genomics staff, including stipends, fellowships, retention allowances and i

Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict . 1.1 C19RM addressed documented gaps in real-time reporting, early warning and surveillance workforce capacity 1.2 The design was technically relevant but insufficiently adapted to state readiness and operating conditions 1.3 The preparedness transition was appropriate, but late operationalisation reduced the window for measurable system gains

Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Coherence: verdict . 2.1 Strategic alignment was strong, but interoperability and data governance remain unresolved 2.2 Partner coordination reduced duplication, but weak contribution traceability and donor dependence remain major risks

Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Effectiveness: verdict . 3.1 SORMAS/IDSR produced the clearest functional gains in real-time reporting 3.2 EBS and CBS extended early warning, but functionality and maturity remain uneven
Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Efficiency: verdict . 4.1 Procurement, sequencing and recurrent-cost gaps reduced value realisation

Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Impact: verdict . 5.1 C19RM’s strongest contribution was improved reporting visibility, completeness and timeliness 5.2 Against the external IHR benchmark, the 2023 JEE records surveillance at developed-to-demonstrated capacity, a result C19RM shares with other cont…

Module 05 Surveillance System Strengthening — OECD-DAC Sustainability: verdict . 6.1 Financial sustainability: recurrent-cost ownership has not yet shifted to government 6.2 Operational sustainability: state ownership and routine financing are emerging but uneven 6.3 Workforce sustainability: continuity matters as much as equipment

Module 06 — Community Systems Strengthening and Risk Communication and Community Engagement. Theory-based contribution analysis against the OECD-DAC criteria, with Equity and System Risk applied as cross-cutting lenses. Module 6: Community Systems Strengthening and Risk Communication and Community Engagement

Module 06 Community Systems Strengthening and Risk Communication and Community Engagement — OECD-DAC Relevance: verdict Strong. Whether the investment responded to real, pre-existing need at national, state, and facility levels; whether its design reflected heterogeneity across states, levels of care, and population groups, including hard-to-reach communities; and whether priorities evolved from acute response toward durable… C19RM responded to a real and consistently evidenced need: widespread community distrust in an underperforming primary health system, unprotected health workers when the pandemic hit, a key-population protection emergency under lockdown, an… 1.1 The starting gap was widespread community distrust in underperforming primary health-care services and weak community engagement systems 1.2 The design reflected heterogeneity by population group and channel, and was weakest on hard-to-reach communities 1.3 Priorities evolved from acute response toward durable preparedness, with timeliness that was responsive but not yet ahead of outbreaks

Module 07 Infection Prevention and Control Systems Strengthening — Investment delivered: Convergence: The two streams converge strongly on EQ1: both document a pre-investment baseline of missing knowledge, scarce commodities, non-compliant infrastructure and unrecognised HAI burden, and both record improvement across these dimensions. • Survey signal: IIRMS (n=196-212): 78% of assessed sites received basic IPC items (gloves, masks, ABHR); 75% received IPC programme support (hand hygiene, training); 30% received renovation. IPCAF-MR (n=165 tertiary/secondary): CC5 Multimodal Strategies met by 76.3% of federal tertiary facilities; CC1 IPC Programmes met by only 10.5%; CC3 Education/Training met by 44.7% (tertiary), 25.2% (sub-national). HAI PPS (April-June 2025, n=1,177 patients): 3.7% pooled HAI prevalence; 71% antimicrobial use; 51.4% Watch-category antibiotics. JEE: 2017 P.3.3 score 2; 2019 P.3.3 score 1 (JEE 2.0); 2023 R4.1=3, R4.2=2, R4.3=1. SPAR IPC capacity: 33/100 (2022), 40/100 (2023), 47/100 (2024), 60/100 (2025), a 27-point gain over the period (see Table 7.2 and Figure 7.2). • Alternatives tested: Alternative 1 (pre-existing programme sufficient): tested and rejected. • Rating: STRONG. The investment responded to a documented, internationally verified infection-prevention crisis at every level of Nigeria's health system. The pre-investment baseline is confirmed by four independent international assessments (2017 JEE, 2017 WASH-FIT, 2023 JEE, 2023 SPAR). The IIRMS corroborates KII accounts on commodity and hand-hygiene gains. The principal relevance limitation is t

Module 08 Health Products, Supply Chain and Waste Management — Investment delivered: Pharmaceutical-grade warehouses: 22 pharmaceutical-grade state warehouses across 21 states upgraded and operationalised (loading bays, racking, cold chain, inventory); the Abuja Premier Medical Warehouse was expanded separately; and a national warehouse performance framework • Logistics information systems: Integrated NHLMIS and ePOD training for end users, with supply-chain maturity modelling and QMS accreditation readiness • Last-mile distribution: Integrated last-mile distribution through third- and fourth-party logistics (3PL/4PL), and motorcycles procured for delivery to lower-tier facilities • Healthcare waste management: Inter-ministerial working group reactivated; national HCWM assessment across 12 states and the FCT, informing the revised National HCWM Policy and Strategic Plan 2024 to 2028; segregation, sharps-safety and treatment-site practice (redirected from incinerator procurement after WHO engagement) • State drug management agencies: DMA model supported to anchor state ownership and the sustainability plan

**6.12 ATM Service Continuity and Mitigation**[^c1]
*Implementation architecture · how approved investments became delivery across 36 states and the FCT**↑ Back to Pillar Index*

Nigeria’s COVID-19 Response Mechanism (C19RM) investment was of a scale, technical complexity, and geographic scope that required a lot of rigor and intensity to manage Project activities. The programme was delivered in an emergency environment where timeliness was paramount, systems faced considerable operational pressures, and decision-making frequently required action despite evolving information and less-than-optimal implementation conditions. The portfolio had to reach federal agencies, state systems, facilities, laboratories, warehouses, communities and campaign sites across the country, while coordinating a vast array of actors with different mandates, financing channels, technical strengths and implementation responsibilities. The C19RM grant officially commenced on 1 April 2021, at a moment when Nigeria needed more than emergency commodities. The response had to protect HIV, TB and malaria services, expand diagnostics, strengthen oxygen systems, support surveillance, improve infection prevention and control, maintain malaria campaigns, protect community systems, move health products, strengthen warehousing, improve quality of care and prepare the country for future health emergencies. This was not a simple delivery chain. Principal Recipients were responsible for fiduciary and grant accountability. Co-PR and disease-programme platforms had to carry technically specialized investments. Sub-Recipients and departmental implementation channels were responsible for translating priorities into work plans, extending delivery of agreed interventions into states and communi

The C19RM project was the mechanism through which approved investments became oxygen plants, GeneXpert laboratories, Truenat platforms, digital X-rays, warehouse upgrades, SORMAS devices, community-led monitoring systems, malaria ICT4D campaigns, IPC structures, RCCE platforms, dashboards, workforce capacity and service-continuity models. At the centre sat NACA as the primary Principal Recipient and accountability backbone for much of the portfolio. Alongside NACA, NTBLCP served as a major co-PR and delivery route for TB-linked, laboratory, oxygen and health-product investments. Lagos State Ministry of Health offered a distinct subnational PR example, showing how a high-capacity cosmopolitan state could connect grant management more directly to implementation realities. Around these structures sat sub-recipients such as NCDC, NMEP, NTBLCP, FHI 360, IHVN and Civil Society Organizations. There were also Government Partners such as NPHCDA, NPSCMP and FMOH departments; implementation partners such as FHI360, IHVN, CRS and others; specialist organizations such as CHAI, WHO, UNICEF, Georgetown University, Data.FI, Jhpiego and ICAP; and community actors who helped translate national investments into frontline action. When the landscape worked, money, technical quality, implementation speed and accountability moved together. When agreements, procurement, cash flow, role clarity or operational readiness lagged, implementation slowed. Understanding this delivery landscape is therefore essential to understanding how one of Nigeria’s largest health-security investments was translated i

t: NACA — the main C19RM grant spine
x: NACA was the main Principal Recipient for the C19RM portfolio. Its role was not simply administrative. NACA provided funding to Sub-Recipients, carried accountability for grant issues, coordinated activities across SRs, monitored and supervised implementation, verified deliverables and handled procurement. For the EOP narrative, NACA should be read as the main grant spine: the institution through which a large part of the C19RM financing, contracting, oversight, procurement and accountability moved. In the consolidated portfolio architecture, NACA carried approximately US$238.434 million of the C19RM investment. This placed NACA at the centre of fund flow and implementation oversight across several major workstreams, including surveillance, RCCE, IPC, oxygen, health products, community systems, programme management, supply-chain coordination and partner engagement. The strength of this role was that it created a single accountability backbone for a very large and complex portfolio. The pressure was that a high volume of technical work depended on the responsiveness of PR systems. Where approvals, documentation, procurement and disbursement moved well, implementation advanced. Where they slowed, several pillars felt the effect. This is one of the most important architecture lessons from C19RM. In an emergency and preparedness portfolio, the PR function must protect fiduciary integrity, but it must also enable response speed. Accountability without speed weakens emergency delivery. Speed without accountability creates risk. The strongest model is one that protects both.

NTBLCP carried a major co-PR and disease-programme implementation role. Its C19RM additional funding architecture placed NTBLCP as Principal Recipient for approximately US$78.812 million, with SRs carrying approximately US$52.252 million, within a total NTBLCP-linked envelope of approximately US$131.065 million. This made NTBLCP one of the most important delivery routes for TB-linked, oxygen-linked, supply-chain, laboratory and health-systems investments. NTBLCP’s role was especially important because COVID-19 disrupted TB diagnosis and case notification. GeneXpert testing volumes decreased during lockdowns, presumptive TB generation declined, TB notification dropped, DR-TB access was disrupted and diagnostic platforms came under pressure. The response could not be managed as a generic emergency activity. It needed the TB programme’s technical mandate, facility network, diagnostic systems, state TB structures and implementation experience. Through the NTBLCP-linked architecture, C19RM supported GeneXpert cartridges, GeneXpert laboratory optimization, Truenat deployment, digital X-rays, TB-LAMP, DR-TB decentralization tools, oxygen piping, warehouse and public-health storage strengthening, laboratory-linked systems investments and PHC-renovations, all routed through relevant national structures such as NPHCDA and programme-linked delivery channels. The strategic lesson is clear: disease programmes were not peripheral to pandemic preparedness. HIV, TB and malaria platforms became part of the preparedness infrastructure. C19RM worked best where it protected those platforms and

t: Lagos State Ministry of Health — a unique subnational PR initiative
x: Lagos State Ministry of Health was a unique subnational PR example. Under the earlier C19RM 2020 arrangement, Lagos carried a dedicated allocation of approximately US$5.436 million, while also operating within its broader Global Fund PR architecture for TB, HIV and health-systems implementation. This matters because Lagos was not only a beneficiary state. It was the national COVID-19 epicentre and one of the most complex service-delivery environments in the country. The Lagos PR model showed that, where state capacity exists, a subnational PR can connect financing and implementation more directly to state systems, facilities, service-delivery adaptations, EMR deployment, health insurance platforms, TB sample movement, community pharmacies and differentiated service delivery. During COVID-19, Lagos adapted through ARV pick-up points, community pharmacies, multi-month dispensing, case management, TB sample transfer using bike riders, virtual procurement processes, continuous PPE distribution, and health-system digital investments. Its state-level grant management also connected to CarePay, health insurance, EMR, TB/HIV performance and COVID-19 mitigation. This is not just a Lagos story. It is a lesson about subnational systems. When states have the right capacity, they can adapt quickly and manage complex service-continuity challenges. Lagos shows that state-level PR or PR-like capacity can be valuable for future health-security investments, especially in high-burden states with strong institutional infrastructure.

t: 3. The Sub-Recipient and Government Partners Implementation Layer
x: The NACA-NMEP agreement is a useful example of how this layer worked. NACA engaged the Federal Ministry of Health through NMEP to implement mitigation for malaria programmes for COVID-19. The agreement defined the scope, workplan, budget, project term, disbursement conditions, compliance expectations, audit requirements and reporting responsibilities. In practical terms, this shows that technical intent alone did not trigger implementation. It had to be translated into a legal, financial and operational agreement.

t: 4. Spotlight on NCDC: The Technical Agency at the Centre of Public Health Preparedness
x: NCDC deserves specific treatment because it sat at the centre of several C19RM preparedness functions. Its role cut across surveillance, SORMAS, Event-Based Surveillance, Community-Based Surveillance, IDSR, PHEOCs, RCCE, IPC, public health laboratories, genomic sequencing and emergency coordination. NCDC was not simply another implementation actor. It was the national public health agency responsible for several of the systems C19RM was trying to strengthen. The surveillance investments depended on NCDC’s technical leadership for SORMAS, EBS, CBS and IDSR. The IPC work depended on NCDC’s IPC Division and National IPC Programme. RCCE activities depended on NCDC risk communication and health promotion structures. PHEOC and emergency-management work depended on NCDC’s role in national and subnational public health emergency coordination. This made NCDC both a delivery actor and a sustainability owner. Many C19RM-supported systems will only retain value if they are absorbed into NCDC’s routine platforms, staffing, data systems, supervision structures and preparedness functions. The NCDC experience also revealed a central architecture tension. Technical agencies can carry urgent response responsibility, but if they do not control the activity cash flow, procurement timing or operational approvals needed to move quickly, emergency response can be slow. This does not argue against fiduciary controls. It argues for emergency grant arrangements that give technical agencies enough operating speed while preserving PR accountability.

t: 5. The TSO and TA Layer: Funded Implementation Support and Complementary Problem-Solving Capacity
x: Technical Support Organizations (TSOs) and Technical Assistance (TAs) partners were both critical to C19RM delivery, but they did not always enter the architecture in the same way. Some organizations received C19RM funding directly or through PR/SR arrangements to implement defined workstreams. These were largely Technical Support Organizations or implementation partners. Others supported C19RM through complementary funding sources, including USG-funded technical assistance mechanisms such as Project STELLAR, FHI 360 EpiC, Data.FI, ICAP, Georgetown, and Jhpiego-linked TA arrangements. These partners strengthened the same C19RM workstreams, but their support did not always sit directly inside the C19RM grant budget. This distinction matters because it helps explain how the portfolio actually functioned. C19RM financing paid for many of the core implementation activities and partner workstreams. Complementary TA helped the system move faster, solve bottlenecks, improve quality, strengthen documentation, support PMU functions, and transfer capability into national and state systems. Technical Support Organizations and Technical Assistance partners were therefore not a single undifferentiated partner category. Some were implementation partners. Some were specialist technical organizations. Some provided embedded support. Some worked through USG technical assistance arrangements. Some supported PMU functions. Some supported technical teams directly. Some did several of these at once. In C19RM, TA often operated as problem-solving support inside the delivery system. Sometimes it

t: 5. The TSO and TA Layer: Funded Implementation Support and Complementary Problem-Solving Capacity
x: The EpiC implementation update shows this clearly. Its TA to NACA, NCDC and CCM included embedded staff, grant management support , procurement support, PR/SR review preparation, CCM website activation, communication strategy support, programme monitoring and tracking tools and capacity strengthening. This was not disease-specific TA. It was portfolio operations support. Overall, the essence of the TA was to solve immediate implementation problems, build the capacity of the people who would continue the work and leave behind tools, routines or platforms that could be used after the project ended.

t: 6. USG Technical Assistance: The Acceleration and Problem-Solving Layer
x: The USG-supported TA layer needs to be visible because it played a distinct role in implementation acceleration, technical depth and systems problem-solving. It was not always a grant implementer in the formal PR/SR sense, but it helped the portfolio move forward and make tangible progress. The USG partner coordination model brought together ICAP, Georgetown University, Data.FI, FHI 360 EpiC and Jhpiego, each supporting different parts of the portfolio. This was a networked TA model. It did not sit neatly under one pillar. It worked across surveillance, emergency management, case management, RCCE, oxygen, supply chain, laboratories and programme management. Its value was not only technical advice. It helped the PR, SRs, PMU and technical teams solve practical problems that slow implementation: dashboards, SOPs, systems, training, costing, data flow, bottleneck tracking, asset verification and coordination. The lesson is that TA should be treated as part of the implementation architecture, not as an add-on. Where TA was well matched to the problem, it accelerated delivery and improved technical quality. Where coordination was weak, multiple TA streams could become fragmented.

Malaria ICT4D: A Clear Example of Architecture in Motion**[^c5] The malaria ICT4D work is one of the clearest examples of how the architecture actually behaved. NMEP and CRS prepared malaria documents submitted to the Global Fund. NMEP was to work on the Malaria Indicator Survey and ICT4D for SMC. CRS was to work on ICT4D for LLIN campaigns and digital platform accessibility. Chemonics was linked to logistics. But before implementation could move, NACA needed to formalize CRS engagement through an MoU, CRS required dedicated finance and ICT support, Chemonics engagement required discussion with the Global Fund Country Team, and ICT4D procurement had a three- to four-month lead time.:O This example shows why implementation architecture matters. The technical idea was strong, but it still needed NMEP technical leadership, CRS digital and campaign support, Chemonics logistics, NACA contracting, Global Fund Country T

t: 8. TA Models: How Technical Support Actually Worked
x: This was the architecture in real life. It was not a straight line from approval to activity. It was a chain of technical intent, formal engagement, procurement, staffing, logistics, cost adjustment, and field execution. **10. ****HIV Mitigation: Using Existing Disease Platforms to Protect Continuity**[^c6] The HIV mitigation architecture also shows why existing programme systems matter. COVID-19 created fear of facility attendance, disrupted access to OSS and facilities, reduced HRH availability, increased client wait times, affected transport access and increased pressure on laboratories. Pre-implementation analysis showed major linkage, retention, viral load and PrEP continuation gaps. C19RM responded by using FHI 360 as SR to NACA and AHNI, ECEWS and SFH as downstream implementing partners in the HIV mitigation architecture. This allowed the grant to move through existing HIV structures. The model supported innovative approaches to sustaining service delivery through existing platforms; community outreach, CBO delivery, differentiated ART, home delivery, MMD, community pharmacy refills, virtual case management, appointment booking, telemedicine, ECHO platforms, situation rooms and caregiver forums for children and adolescents living with HIV. The strategic insight is that HIV mitigation was not only a set of activities. It was a service-continuity architecture. It showed how a disease programme can protect clients during an emergency by shifting care closer to communities and using digital tools to maintain follow-up.

National scale with technical differentiation. C19RM was national in scope, but each pillar required a different technical pathway. Surveillance moved through NCDC, SORMAS, IDSR, EBS, CBS and PHEOCs. Oxygen moved through NACA, FMOH/DHS, the Oxygen Desk, CHAI, WHO, UNICEF, FHI360, states and facilities. Laboratory systems moved through NACA, NTBLCP, NCDC, MLSD, IHVN, NIPOST, reference laboratories and technical partners. Supply chain and health products and waste management moved through NACA, NTBLCP, NPSCMP, FMoEnv, FMoH, UNICEF, Chemonics/MEBS, state DMAs and logistics partners. Existing platforms instead of parallel systems. C19RM used existing HIV, TB, malaria, surveillance, community and supply-chain platforms. That reduced duplication and allowed emergency financing to move faster. Technical support closer to implementation bottlenecks. TSOs and TA partners helped solve practical problems: oxygen tracking, payment delays, warehouse SOPs, PR/SR reviews, SORMAS backend work, EBS rollout, malaria ICT4D, GeneXpert optimization, asset verification and case-management dashboards. A bridge between emergency response and future preparedness. C19RM helped build emergency and preparedness assets that could be sustained and extended through successor investments: laboratory strengthening, supply chain, surveillance, oxygen, community systems, quality improvement and financial risk protection. **14. ****Challenges

Fiduciary control and emergency speed were sometimes in contention. The early C19RM implementation experience showed that fiduciary control and emergency speed did not always move at the same pace. Where technical agencies were responsible for response delivery but did not directly control activity cash flow, emergency implementation could slow. The lesson is not that fiduciary control was unnecessary. It is that emergency grants need financing pathways that protect accountability while still allowing technical agencies to act with the speed required during outbreaks. Procurement timelines shaped implementation reality. Several activities depended on procurement lead times: ICT4D devices, oxygen equipment, laboratory platforms, digital tools, warehouse inputs and commodities. Technical plans could only become implementation when procurement, contracting and delivery timelines aligned. Technical quality varied by absorptive capacity. Some states absorbed new tools and systems quickly. Others required more support. Sustainability will depend on whether state structures finance and manage what C19RM Project implemented. TA and TSO roles are sometimes blurred. In practice, the distinction between TSO and TA was not always clear Some organizations provided both implementation support and technical assistance. Sustainability is not automatic. Systems created through emergency financing do not sustain themselves. Oxygen desks, PHEOCs, SORMAS, eN-LIS, CLM, warehouse systems, dashboards, digital campaign tools and training platforms all require recurrent financing, ownership and rou

**Clarify institutional ownership.** Every system output should have a named owner: NCDC for surveillance and public health emergency systems; FMOH/DHS and oxygen governance structures for oxygen; NTBLCP and laboratory actors for TB and diagnostics; NMEP for malaria campaign systems; NASCP/NTPP for HIV service-continuity platforms; NPSCMP and relevant supply-chain structures for warehousing and logistics; and state ministries for state-level absorption. **Move from project committees to routine governance.** TWGs, PHEOCs, oxygen desks, laboratory quality structures, RCCE platforms, supply-chain forums and CLM review structures should become routine governance mechanisms, not project-era meetings. **Budget for recurrent functions.** The systems created under C19RM require recurrent costs: device replacement, data bundles, software hosting, oxygen maintenance, warehouse operations, supervision, refresher training, dashboard management, asset verification, community monitoring and coordination meetings. **Protect PMU and coordination capacity.** Payment tracking, filing systems, grant templates, PR/SR reviews, asset registers and issue escalation should remain part of routine grant management practice. **Strengthen state-level absorption.** States need clearer roles, budget lines and performance expectations for systems introduced under C19RM. Subnational ownership should be deliberately built, not assumed. **Keep TA transitional and capability-focused.** TA should have clear handover milestones. It should not create indefinite dependence. Its purpose should be to transfer cap

*Figure 3. The sustainability pathway from project delivery to routine institutional ownership.* **16. Strategic Lessons**[^c8] Architecture is implementation. The PR/SR/Sub-SR/TSO/TA structure directly shaped delivery speed, quality, adaptation and sustainability. Existing disease platforms are preparedness assets. HIV, TB and malaria systems carried emergency adaptations and should be treated as part of the national preparedness base. Subnational PR capacity has strategic value. Lagos shows that strong states can manage complex adaptation and should not always be treated only as downstream implementation sites. TA must be matched to the bottleneck. Some bottlenecks needed technical advice. Others needed embedded support, PMU optimization, digital support, implementation support, procurement support or field supervision. Partner contribution should be explicit. A good EOP narrative should show who designed, who implemented, who optimized, who trained, who digitized, who supervised and who helped sustain. Close-out should simplify ownership. A complex delivery architecture should transition into clearer routine ownership: named institutions, named budget lines, named platforms, named maintenance responsibilities and named review structures.

The C19RM architecture combined grant stewardship, disease-programme platforms, specialist support, technical assistance, field delivery and state/community reach. It was not perfect. It had friction, delays, overlaps and uneven absorption. But it gave Nigeria an operating model for moving a large, multi-pillar health-security investment across institutions and geographies. The real legacy is not the organogram. It is the lesson that complex health-security investments need an architecture that can hold four things together at once: money, technical quality, implementation speed and institutional ownership. That is the architecture Nigeria now needs to sustain.

*Turning Emergency Funds into Preparedness Systems**↑ Back to Pillar Index* Nigeria’s C19RM investment story begins with the mechanisms that carried the financing. The portfolio did not move through one institution, one disease programme or one delivery channel. It moved through a national grant architecture anchored by NACA and NTBLCP, with Lagos State Ministry of Health carrying a distinct state PR window in a high-burden implementation environment. Together, the three mapped streams represent US$374,254,640 in C19RM-related investment: US$238,484,000 through NACA, US$130,754,000 through NTBLCP and US$5,016,640 through the Lagos State Ministry of Health C19RM window. The figures point to three different investment roles. NACA carried the largest national systems platform, spanning broad RSSH functions, community systems, IPC-linked procurement, oxygen-related procurement, governance and cross-cutting health-system reinforcement. NTBLCP carried a wider implementation platform than TB mitigation alone. Its role cut across TB service continuity, diagnostics, laboratory systems, GeneXpert and Truenat optimization, bidirectional testing, oxygen-piping expansion, supply-chain and warehouse-linked investments, and multi-disease service-continuity systems. Lagos State Ministry of Health carried a high-burden state PR window where COVID-19 mitigation, TB/HIV continuity, EMR, laboratory systems, quality improvement and RSSH investments intersected. The C19RM implementation period ran from 1 April 2021 to 31 December 2025. Activities in 2026 are best understood as documentation, eva

C19RM did not remove all fragility from Nigeria’s health system. It helped finance the machinery Nigeria needed to respond under pressure, protect essential disease services and leave behind a broader base of preparedness capability.The investment architecture was clear: emergency funds entered the system under pressure, scaled into response infrastructure, and then pivoted toward preparedness systems.

The C19RM portfolio evolved through three investment phases. These were not only timeline markers; they reflected shifts in what the grant was trying to do. | **Phase** | **Investment job** | **Main intent** | | --- | --- | --- | | **Emergency Response** | Stop the bleeding | Move emergency commodities, diagnostics, PPE, oxygen support and surge tools into the system quickly. | | **Fast-Track Scale-Up** | Industrialise the response | Expand oxygen, laboratories, surveillance, PHEOCs, critical care, AgRDTs and digital reporting systems. | | **Pivot to Pandemic Preparedness / RSSH** | Build durable systems | Move from emergency response into warehouses, HMIS, IPC institutionalization, QMS, community systems, ATM mitigation and preparedness capability. | The first phase responded to immediate pressure. The second built response infrastructure. The third turned the portfolio toward the systems Nigeria would need after the emergency had receded. *Figure 5. The three investment phases, from emergency response to preparedness.*

The first phase was defined by speed. Nigeria needed to protect health workers, expand testing, keep facilities safe, support case management and reduce the risk that COVID-19 would disrupt HIV, TB and malaria services. Investment emphasis centred on PPE, AgRDTs and PCR-related commodities, oxygen concentrators, pulse oximeters, ambulances, mobile clinics, GeneXpert optimization, specimen-transport materials, data loggers and rapid facility support. This was the pressure phase. Its value lay in how quickly essential response tools could enter the system and reduce immediate operational risk.

The second phase changed the character of the portfolio. The grant was no longer only moving emergency supplies. It was building the machinery required to respond at national scale. This phase industrialised oxygen, diagnostics, surveillance and emergency coordination. Investments included PSA plants, LOX tanks, oxygen cylinders, oxygen concentrators, GeneXpert and Truenat optimization, next-generation sequencing, AgRDT cascade expansion, SORMAS deployment, Event-Based Surveillance and PHEOC scale-up. The shift was important. Nigeria was not only buying inputs; it was building the operating spine for a pandemic response.

The third phase was less visible but more strategic. As acute COVID-19 pressure declined, the portfolio moved into the systems that would determine whether emergency assets could outlast the pandemic. Investments moved toward warehouse upgrades, NHLMIS, APMW, 4PL last-mile distribution, eN-LIS, QMS accreditation preparation, biomedical engineering capacity, Strengthening National Healthcare Waste Management systems, IPC institutionalization, HMIS and digital health, Open Data Commons, DHIS2/NDR integration, OpenMRS pilots, tablets for facility data capture, community-led monitoring, RCCE, bidirectional COVID/ATM testing, multi-month dispensing and quality improvement. This was where the portfolio’s deeper value became clearer. The strongest legacy was not only the most visible equipment. It was the data systems, maintenance capacity, inventory discipline, trained users, community feedback loops, quality systems and governance routines that could turn emergency investments into preparedness systems.
The investment story sits around a systems stack rather than a single pillar.

| **Investment area** | **What the money became** | **Strategic meaning** | | --- | --- | --- | | **Oxygen and respiratory care** | PSA plants, LOX tanks, cylinders, concentrators, pulse oximeters, piping, biomedical engineering capacity and oxygen coordination structures. | Oxygen shifted from emergency commodity to preparedness infrastructure. | | **Laboratory systems and diagnostics** | GeneXpert optimization, new GeneXpert units, Truenat, NGS, eN-LIS, QMS, specimen referral and laboratory upgrades. | Diagnostics became a multi-disease backbone for COVID-19, TB and future outbreaks. | | **Surveillance and PHEOCs** | IDSR, SORMAS, Event-Based Surveillance, Community-Based Surveillance, PHEOC equipment and emergency management tools. | Outbreak intelligence and coordination gained a stronger operating spine. | | **IPC** | Facility support, IPC workforce development, diploma pathway, mentorship networks, REDCap, HAI surveillance and state IPC structures. | IPC moved beyond PPE into workforce, data and facility practice. | | **Health Products,Supply chain and logistics** | Procurement of Health commodities and equipment, Warehouse upgrades, NHLMIS, APMW, 4PL Last mile distribution and warehouse staff training. | Commodity movement became a preparedness function, not just logistics. | | **HMIS and digital health** | Tablets, Open Data Commons, DHIS2/NDR integration, OpenMRS pilots and reporting tools. | Data visibility became part of emergency readiness and programme management. | | **Community systems and ****RCCE**[^c9] | ACOMIN, NEPWHAN, NLC workplace programming, CLM and
disruption. | | **Quality improvement** | QMS, facility readiness, mentorship, standards, supervision and data-use routines. | Quality became part of preparedness, not an afterthought. |

Nigeria's C19RM portfolio was not difficult because it was large alone; it was difficult because it had to make a crowded emergency system act with enough coherence to deliver. The COVID-19 Response Mechanism (C19RM) project in Nigeria was operationalized through a multi-tiered, multi-stakeholder governance and implementation architecture aimed at enhancing national pandemic preparedness and response capacity, strengthening health systems resilience, and ensuring the uninterrupted delivery and sustainability of essential HIV, TB, and malaria (HTM) services. The programme leveraged coordinated engagement among government institutions, development partners, implementing entities, civil society organizations (CSOs), and community-based structures to facilitate integrated service delivery, risk mitigation, resource optimization, and adaptive programme management within the broader health systems strengthening (HSS) framework. The pillar provided overall stewardship, fiduciary oversight, strategic direction, operational coordination, performance monitoring, and accountability mechanisms necessary for the effective and efficient implementation of programme interventions in line with national response priorities and Global Fund grant management requirements.Financing had to move through Principal Recipients, Sub-Recipients, national disease programmes, federal departments, technical partners, civil society networks, procurement channels, state structures and facility-level delivery points at a time when COVID-19 was still distorting normal operating routines. Programme Management,

This was the ground on which the rest of the investment stood. Surveillance, laboratories, IPC, oxygen, supply chain, community systems, HMIS and service-continuity interventions could only become a portfolio because there was an architecture for assigning responsibility, tracking performance, escalating bottlenecks and maintaining fiduciary discipline. The pillar's importance was most visible where implementation complexity was highest: when procurement slowed, when partner roles overlapped, when national priorities had to be reconciled with grant requirements, when the single-PR arrangement became too heavy, and when the portfolio had to adapt into a dual-PR model. Its value was not that it removed all friction. Its value was that it gave Nigeria a mechanism for governing that friction without losing the strategic direction of the investment. | **Narrative centre** The governance pillar carried the system behind the delivery. It converted a large, complex, multi-actor emergency investment into a managed national platform with stewardship, accountability, procurement control, partner coordination and implementation visibility. | | --- |

Across the emergency phase, governance was primarily about control under pressure. The first requirement was to keep the grant moving while protecting essential HIV, TB and malaria services and responding to urgent COVID-19 needs. NACA's initial role as sole Principal Recipient created a central point for stewardship, procurement governance, financial oversight, implementation reporting and coordination across Sub-Recipients and government institutions. That arrangement made sense in the early shock because speed and control were paramount. But it also revealed the limits of trying to carry a highly technical, multi-disease, multi-actor national investment through one operational channel. The rapid scale-up and systems-strengthening phases were therefore marked by adaptation. As procurement pressure, absorption demands and TB-related implementation complexity increased, the portfolio moved from single-PR control toward a more distributed operating model, with NTBLCP brought in as an additional Principal Recipient for key TB and health-systems investments. This transition was one of the clearest governance lessons of C19RM: architecture has to evolve as the investment evolves. By the later phase, the governance task had shifted from emergency coordination to institutional delivery discipline - clarifying roles, strengthening accountability, aligning technical workstreams, documenting partner contribution, and preparing the portfolio for sustainability, transition and future pandemic-preparedness financing.

| **Phase** | **Governance pressure** | **Core response** | **What changed** | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | **Emergency response** | Speed, uncertainty, urgent procurement and continuity of HTM services. | Single-PR national control through NACA, emergency coordination, early SR mobilisation and consolidated reporting. | A central grant-management spine was created for emergency delivery. | | **Rapid scale-up** | More actors, larger procurements, technical complexity, absorption pressure and specialised disease-programme needs. | Implementation restructuring and introduction of NTBLCP as additional PR for TB-related and associated investments. | The architecture shifted from centralised control to differentiated delivery. | | **Systems strengthening** | Sustainability, transition, evidence documentation, asset ownership and preparedness alignment. | Cross-pillar coordination, partner contribution mapping, routine oversight, performance review and handover logic. | Governance became an institutional platform for future RSSH-PPR investment. | *Figure 7. Governance evolved with the investment, from single-PR control to an institutional platform.*
