The JEE lens sharpens the surveillance story. Nigeria had surveillance capacity before C19RM, but the investment helped deepen the system where maturity still depended on digitization, workforce use, subnational functionality, community signals and faster response. The final test is whether these gains become routine, financed and interoperable across all states and sectors.

The surveillance pillar made a visible shift toward a more digital and layered early warning architecture, but several constraints still affected how fully the system could function and how durable the gains would be after the grant period. These challenges do not reduce the value of the investment; they clarify where the next phase of work must focus. SORMAS adoption was broad, but routine use still needed deeper institutionalization. Although SORMAS was widely deployed, routine utilization varied across facilities because of user proficiency gaps, parallel paper reporting, supervision gaps, connectivity constraints and limited institutionalization of electronic workflows. In some settings, SORMAS functioned as an additional reporting channel rather than the primary workflow for surveillance. Sustaining the platform will therefore require routine adoption checks, user-support systems, supervision, and a clear transition away from duplicate paper processes where digital reporting is functional. Data quality remained the next frontier of digital surveillance. The move to electronic reporting improved speed and visibility, but data quality gaps persisted. Incomplete reporting, delayed data entry, inconsistent validation and documentation errors continued to affect the reliability and usability of surveillance information. The next phase must move beyond whether reports are submitted to whether they are complete, accurate, timely, validated and useful for local action.

Interoperability remained early-stage and limited the full value of digital investment. SORMAS-DHIS2 integration had begun, but seamless interoperability across surveillance, laboratory and routine health information systems was not yet fully achieved. Without stronger integration, programme teams may continue to face parallel reporting processes, fragmented data flows and avoidable delays in analysis. Interoperability is therefore not a technical luxury; it is central to making surveillance information actionable across programmes and levels. Digital infrastructure constrained real-time reporting in weaker settings. Weak internet connectivity, unstable power, device malfunction and insufficient ICT infrastructure affected reporting in remote and resource-limited settings. These constraints were especially important because surveillance performance depends on the ability to report from where events occur, not only from better-resourced facilities. Devices and platforms must therefore be matched with connectivity, power backup, maintenance arrangements and replacement protocols. EBS and CBS coverage remained important but still incomplete. EBS reached 12 states and CBS was piloted in three states, but many communities and states still lacked fully functional decentralized epidemic intelligence and community surveillance systems. This means that the national early warning architecture became more sensitive, but not yet uniformly sensitive. The next phase should expand EBS and CBS based on risk, geography, outbreak history and access constraints, while retaining quality and su

Private-sector reporting remained a major blind spot. Private hospitals, clinics, pharmacies and PPMVs were not yet fully integrated into surveillance reporting, limiting the completeness and representativeness of surveillance data. This matters because many Nigerians first seek care outside the public sector. Weak private-sector participation can delay detection of unusual events, especially where private providers are the first point of contact. Feedback mechanisms were still too weak for a learning system. Facilities, LGAs and frontline officers often submitted reports without timely feedback on data quality, investigation outcomes or public health actions taken. This weakens motivation and reduces the practical value of reporting. A strong surveillance system should not only collect data upward; it should return insight downward and sideways so that facilities, LGAs and communities can improve their own practice. Workforce turnover continued to threaten institutional memory. Transfers, attrition, competing duties and limited refresher training weakened retention of surveillance knowledge and consistent platform use. This is particularly important for DSNOs, facility focal persons and surveillance teams who carry routine reporting. A one-time training model cannot sustain performance in a system where staff movement is frequent. Sustainability risks were operational, not only financial. The pillar requires financing for devices, connectivity, platform maintenance, supportive supervision, refresher training, community informant engagement, EBS teams and PHEOC operations.

The next phase of surveillance strengthening should protect the gains already made while moving the system from project-supported scale-up to routine national practice. The priority is not simply to keep platforms alive; it is to make surveillance a functional public health discipline at community, facility, LGA, state and national levels. Make SORMAS integrated and routine. SORMAS should become the default workflow for electronic disease notification, case investigation and outbreak management, with paper reporting progressively reduced where digital systems are functional. This requires clear national and state directives, site-level accountability, routine user support, and periodic audits of platform use. The goal should be simple: surveillance officers and facilities should use SORMAS because it is how surveillance work is done, not because a project requires it. **Complete interoperability with DHIS2** and laboratory systems. SORMAS, DHIS2, laboratory information systems and other reporting platforms should be connected to reduce duplication and strengthen analysis. Interoperability should focus on practical use cases: case notification, laboratory confirmation, outbreak dashboards, reporting completeness, event verification and response monitoring. This will help move Nigeria from multiple digital systems toward a more coherent public health intelligence architecture. **Scale CBS beyond pilot states without losing quality.** The CBS model in Kano, Oyo and Enugu should be refined and expanded to more states, especially underserved, border, conflict-affected, rural and

**Institutionalize EBS at subnational level.** EBS should become a standing state function linked to PHEOCs, RRTs, media monitoring, hotline systems, community alerts and One Health structures. States should have defined personnel, SOPs, verification timelines, escalation pathways and routine reviews of signals. This will keep epidemic intelligence closer to where threats first appear. **Protect LGA DSNO and facility workforce capacity.** Continuous refresher training, mentorship and onboarding are needed to manage staff turnover and preserve surveillance quality. DSNOs and facility focal persons should receive routine coaching on case definitions, SORMAS use, data quality, event verification, community signal management and outbreak thresholds. Workforce sustainability should be treated as a standing cost of surveillance. **Use 7-1-7 as a routine performance tool.** The 7-1-7 framework should be used regularly to review outbreak performance and drive practical improvement. Each outbreak or event should become a learning opportunity: where detection was delayed, where notification slowed, where response bottlenecks emerged, and what must change before the next event. Used well, 7-1-7 can turn surveillance from a reporting system into a performance improvement system. **Bring the private sector into surveillance.** Private facilities, pharmacies, laboratories, patent medicine vendors and informal providers need clear reporting pathways, incentives, training and digital access. Private-sector integration should begin with high-volume providers and high-risk geographies, then

**Close the feedback loop.** Facilities, LGAs and community informants should receive regular feedback on what their reports triggered and how data improved response. Feedback can be simple: weekly bulletins, dashboards, WhatsApp summaries, LGA review meetings, community updates and targeted data-quality notes. This will improve motivation, accountability and learning across the system. **Finance surveillance operations domestically.** Devices, connectivity, supervision, refresher training, CBS informant engagement, EBS teams.Use surveillance data for local decisions, not only national reporting. States and LGAs should routinely use surveillance data to identify hotspots, investigate unusual trends, monitor reporting gaps, plan supervision and target community engagement. The value of surveillance increases when data is used closest to the point where action can be taken. **Treat surveillance as preparedness infrastructure.** Surveillance is not only reporting. It is the country’s early warning system. Sustaining the gains means keeping the people, platforms, communities and response linkages active before the next emergency arrives. Nigeria’s next step is to make this system routine, financed, interoperable and trusted across every layer of the health system.

Risk Communication and Community Engagement was the part of the C19RM response that had to translate public health guidance into trust, understanding and action. During COVID-19, the challenge was not only that people needed information. The harder task was ensuring that information was accurate, timely, culturally resonant, locally trusted and strong enough to influence behaviour in a fast-changing emergency. The RCCE pillar responded to this pressure by moving Nigeria’s public health communication beyond emergency risk messaging toward more localized, data-informed and community-responsive communication practice. The strongest parts of the work were those that connected national guidance to trusted community actors, service-continuity messages, non-pharmaceutical interventions and real-time understanding of community perception. The pillar’s defining value was the shift away from generic national broadcasting toward more targeted communication. Community engagement in Lagos, perception surveys in Cross River and Taraba, cholera polling across 20 states, digital listening, online surveys, “Health Tori” radio drama, translated comics and short field guides all pointed in the same direction: communication had to be grounded in what people were actually hearing, believing, fearing and doing. C19RM helped strengthen the systems behind that shift. The pillar supported the National RCCE Strategy, emergency communication coordination through the EOC structure, subnational capacity assessments, social media and infodemic management training, polling dashboards, e-learning tools, l

The pillar’s centre of gravity sat in emergency response, but its forward legacy is broader: RCCE as a routine public health function that listens, adapts and responds before misinformation, fear or low-risk perception undermines service continuity, outbreak control and community trust.
The RCCE pillar moved through three clear moments: emergency risk communication, localized scale-up, and digital/community systems strengthening.

The first phase focused on immediate communication readiness. The National RCCE Strategy was developed and deployed, and emergency communication structures were activated to respond to the COVID-19 infodemic. Early work focused on rapid message development, public awareness, foundational training and promotion of non-pharmaceutical interventions. This phase helped Nigeria respond quickly to misinformation and rapidly changing public guidance. It also made one weakness clear: national messages alone could not carry the response. Communities needed localized, trusted and repeated communication through actors they knew.

The second phase moved RCCE closer to subnational and community realities. Community-led and community-based organizations were engaged in high-risk settings, especially through the Lagos Community Engagement initiative across 10 high-risk LGAs. Subnational capacity assessments were conducted across 12 states, while teams were trained in social listening, infodemic tracking and online sentiment monitoring. The pillar also began using Kobo Collect for perception and KAP surveys in Cross River and Taraba, helping identify localized misconceptions and information gaps. Targeted campaigns for key vulnerable populations helped counter vaccine hesitancy and reinforce service-continuity messages.

The third phase focused on making RCCE more digital, routine and data-driven. Community polling dashboards were deployed across 20 states, and more than 200 data collectors were trained during the cholera polling scale-up. The e-learning platform linked to Google Classroom helped institutionalize self-paced training, while online polling, social media ad boosts and digital dashboards strengthened rapid feedback loops. The use of disease calendars, community polling, localized drama, translated comics and field guides marked a shift from reactive communication toward more anticipatory outbreak communication. By the close of the cycle, RCCE had begun to operate less as an emergency campaign function and more as a standing public health intelligence and engagement capability.

**National RCCE coordination strengthened.** The National RCCE Strategy was developed and deployed, with emergency communication coordination activated through the national response architecture to support infodemic management, public messaging and misinformation control. **Localized community engagement demonstrated.** The Lagos Community Engagement initiative reached 10 high-risk LGAs through targeted workshops with CSOs and CBOs, showing the value of community-led structures in strengthening local risk perception and field mobilization. **Subnational risk communications capacity assessed.** Capacity and needs assessments were conducted across 12 states, helping identify gaps in local communication systems and informing more context-specific communication support for COVID-19 and other epidemic-prone diseases. **Community perception data strengthened.** Kobo Collect-supported KAP and perception surveys were piloted in Cross River and Taraba, generating localized insight into community knowledge and misconceptions around COVID-19, Lassa Fever and Cholera. **Community polling dashboards expanded.** Community polling dashboards were deployed across 20 states in all six geopolitical zones, integrating field polling, online polls and RCCE community activity reporting for more evidence-informed decision-making. **Infodemic training scaled.** Social Media and Infodemic Management training was completed across 11 of 12 target states, representing a 91.7% execution rate and strengthening the ability of subnational teams to detect, understand and respond to misinformation. **Digita

**Field tools deployed during emergencies.** Short, practical communication tools such as the Cholera Education Guide gave field workers consistent guidance during acute public health events, including flood-associated cholera risks in Maiduguri. **Localized content strengthened.** The pillar supported culturally resonant materials including “Health Tori” radio drama models, translated digital comics and educational content in local languages.

In 2023, NCDC’s Risk Communication Unit, with technical assistance from ICAP, piloted mobile perception surveys in Taraba and Cross River using Kobo Collect. The initial survey captured 807 household responses — 422 in Taraba and 385 in Cross River. The findings showed clear information gaps: 55% of respondents had not received, seen or heard target infectious disease prevention messages in the preceding month. Among those who had received information, radio was the most common source. These insights informed the development of “Health Tori” Season 1, a four-episode radio drama series focused on COVID-19 and Lassa Fever. When conventional broadcast pathways were constrained, the episodes were adapted for NCDC’s digital platforms, supported by targeted social media promotion. The model matured further in 2024 during the cholera polling phase. The project trained over 200 data collectors across 20 states and generated more than 20,000 community responses. The findings identified knowledge gaps around oral rehydration solution preparation and open defecation, directly informing the Cholera Prevention and Control Community Engagement Guide. More than 500 hard copies were distributed across 22 states for use by frontline health educators. The value of this cycle was not only the volume of responses. It showed that RCCE could move from assumption-based messaging to evidence-informed commun

t: 6. State Coverage, Exemplars and Facility Reach
x: RCCE had a national communication footprint, but its deepest operational learning came through targeted state and community work. Cross River and Taraba served as core perception-survey states, generating early KAP and community insight data through mobile-device surveys. Lagos served as an important urban community engagement site, with targeted workshops across 10 high-risk LGAs. Cross River also hosted a major NCDC and ICAP mentorship visit, expanding localized data tracking through its State One Health team and health promotion officers. The cholera polling work expanded the footprint substantially, training over 200 data collectors across 20 states and generating more than 20,000 community responses. Wider assessments and infodemic management activities reached states including Rivers, Plateau, Kebbi, Katsina and Edo, while digital campaigns and polling engaged audiences across Adamawa, Borno, FCT, Kaduna, Kano, Kwara, Niger, Ondo and Oyo. The state footprint shows the pillar’s real value: RCCE worked best where national tools were adapted through state teams, local data and trusted community channels.
The RCCE pillar was delivered through a partnership arrangement that connected grant coordination, national technical leadership, digital systems support and community implementation.

| **Contributor** | **Main Role** | | --- | --- | | **NACA** | Principal Recipient; fund management, budget approval and grant oversight | | **NCDC Risk Communication Unit / Health Promotion Division** | Technical implementation, national RCCE coordination, infodemic management and digital communication | | **ICAP at Columbia University** | Technical assistance for Kobo Collect surveys, state mentoring, LMS and digital learning structures | | **Breakthrough ACTION** | One Health coordination and RCCE technical support | | **CSOs/CBOs** | Community mobilization, field messaging, participatory workshops and localized engagement | | **State Health Promotion Teams** | Subnational adaptation, data collection, risk communication and field coordination |

**NACA — grant coordination and financial oversight.** NACA provided the grant management and approval structure that enabled RCCE activities to move through the C19RM portfolio. **NCDC Risk Communication Unit — national technical leadership.** NCDC led the technical design and delivery of RCCE activities, including infodemic management, digital polling, public messaging, training and the development of localized communication tools. **ICAP — technical assistance for data and learning systems.** ICAP supported Kobo Collect surveys, mentoring of state teams, LMS development and the shift toward more structured digital learning and community polling systems. **CSOs, CBOs and state teams — local trust and field execution.** Community organizations and state health promotion officers helped move communication from national messaging into local settings where behaviour change had to happen.

Together, these contributors helped RCCE move beyond emergency messaging. NACA provided the grant structure. NCDC carried national technical leadership. ICAP strengthened data, learning and mentoring systems. State teams and community actors brought the work closer to real community concerns. The combined result was a more adaptive RCCE platform built around listening, localization, digital feedback and field-ready communication.

**Data-driven messaging design.** Perception and KAP surveys helped ensure that messages were based on what communities actually knew, believed and misunderstood. **Proactive disease-calendar planning.** Aligning polling and content development with the national disease calendar helped move RCCE from reactive messaging toward anticipatory communication. **Blended learning and e-training.** Text and video modules hosted through an LMS and Google Classroom created a more scalable approach to professional development for communication officers. **Social listening for infodemic response.** The use of social media monitoring, online polls and audience analytics helped turn digital platforms into listening tools, not only broadcasting channels. **Short field-ready tools.** The Cholera Education Guide showed the value of concise desk references that field workers could use quickly during active emergencies. **Localized content formats.** Radio drama, digital comics and translated content helped make public health messaging more relatable across different audiences.

**Repeat:** Use community data before designing major campaigns. The strongest communication products were those informed by local perception data. **Redesign:** Move away from one-size-fits-all messaging. Every major campaign should be preceded by state-specific or audience-specific insight gathering. **Institutionalize:** Digital polling, social listening and e-learning should become routine RCCE functions with dedicated budget lines, not temporary project activities. **Strengthen:** State teams need greater ability to analyze and use RCCE data without relying fully on national teams. **Protect:** Communication budgets must reflect modern public health realities, including social media boosting, dashboards, content production and digital infrastructure.

C19RM helped shift RCCE from emergency announcement-making toward a more adaptive, data-informed and locally responsive public health communication system. The pillar strengthened Nigeria’s ability to listen to communities, identify misinformation, understand risk perception, generate targeted content and use digital platforms for rapid feedback. Its value was not only in the messages produced. Its deeper value was the communication machinery it helped strengthen: polling, dashboards, e-learning, social listening, localized content, community actors and field tools. These are now part of Nigeria’s preparedness base.

Nigeria’s Joint External Evaluation provides a useful lens for understanding the RCCE pillar. In 2017, risk communication capacity was uneven: risk communication systems scored 1, while internal coordination, community engagement and rumour management showed stronger but still incomplete capacity. By 2023, RCCE systems, risk communication and community engagement each scored 3, indicating developed capacity, while priority actions still called for subnational ownership, infodemic management, dedicated RCCE budgets and stronger feedback mechanisms. This should not be read as direct attribution. It is better understood as a maturity signal. RCCE moved from fragmented emergency communication toward more developed systems for coordination, community engagement and infodemic response, and C19RM reinforced several of the same areas the JEE identified as priorities.

The JEE lens sharpens the RCCE story. Nigeria had stronger RCCE maturity by 2023, but sustained subnational ownership, financing and feedback systems remained unfinished. C19RM helped reinforce the movement toward a more data-informed and community-responsive communication system.

**State capacity to use RCCE data remained uneven.** Some states had limited capacity to independently analyze and apply RCCE data for campaign design without national support. **Digital gains were vulnerable to infrastructure gaps.** E-learning and online platforms depended on digital literacy, connectivity and basic infrastructure that remained uneven across subnational teams. **Hard-to-reach populations remained difficult to engage consistently.** Qualitative assessments among remote, marginalized or special populations were affected by logistics, access and field-mobilization constraints. **Data management varied across states.** States differed in how RCCE data was collated, stored and reused, limiting the consistency of national communication intelligence. **Field outreach equipment remained weak in some places.** Breakdown of public address vehicles and outreach tools constrained physical campaign reach in rural settings. **Administrative delays disrupted field momentum.** Delayed payments, procurement timelines and daily subsistence rate limitations affected field execution, state confidence and local vendor relationships.

**Make RCCE a standing public health intelligence function.** RCCE should not return to emergency-only messaging. It should remain a routine system for listening, analysing, adapting and communicating before, during and after outbreaks. **Finance digital risk communication directly.** Social listening, online polling, dashboards, ad boosts, content production and LMS hosting need predictable budget lines. Without dedicated funding, the digital infrastructure built under C19RM will weaken. **Strengthen state-level RCCE ownership.** State teams need the tools, budgets and skills to analyze local risk perception and adapt communication without waiting for national teams. **Keep feedback loops active.** Community polling and online feedback should remain part of routine outbreak preparedness so messages are shaped by current public concerns. **Institutionalize e-learning.** The RCCE LMS should become a standing national training platform for health promotion, infodemic management and public health communication. **Protect localization as a core principle.** The strongest RCCE work was localized. Future campaigns should retain state-specific assessments, local languages, trusted actors and culturally relevant formats. **Treat communication as preparedness infrastructure.** Communication is not an accessory to response. It is part of the system that protects trust, risk perception, service continuity and public cooperation during emergencies.
*Phase: Phase 2 — Rapid Scale-Up | Pillar Author: Rahab, Dr. Zahra**↑ Back to Pillar Index*

The C19RM Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics pillar was designed to transform Nigeria's laboratory capacity from an emergency COVID-19 testing response into a more resilient, integrated, and multi-disease diagnostic network. Investments focused on expanding molecular testing capacity, strengthening laboratory infrastructure, improving specimen referral systems, building workforce competencies, enhancing laboratory information management, and institutionalising quality management systems. Key interventions included optimisation of GeneXpert platforms, deployment of 333 Truenat molecular testing units, upgrading of the National Reference Laboratory and selected public health laboratories, establishment of Next Generation Sequencing (NGS) capacity, implementation of the electronic National Laboratory Information System (eN-LIS), accreditation preparedness activities, and strengthening of specimen referral systems. Nationwide training programmes on molecular diagnostics, antigen-based rapid diagnostic testing, biosafety, quality assurance, and laboratory data management complemented these investments and strengthened the capacity of laboratory personnel across the country. At the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, Nigeria's laboratory system faced significant limitations, including restricted molecular testing capacity, fragmented specimen transport systems, limited quality management structures, inadequate workforce capacity, and the absence of an integrated laboratory information system. C19RM investments addressed these gaps through a tiered laboratory network app

A defining feature of the pillar was the use of existing laboratory infrastructure for multiple priority diseases. GeneXpert platforms and associated laboratory networks were leveraged to support COVID-19 testing alongside tuberculosis and HIV viral load services, maximising efficiency and ensuring that investments made during the pandemic strengthened broader disease control programmes. By the end of implementation, laboratory assets and capacities established under C19RM had become part of a broader diagnostic platform supporting surveillance, outbreak response, and routine testing beyond COVID-19. The pillar also initiated longer-term system strengthening efforts aimed at improving sustainability and integration. These included the phased rollout of eN-LIS to improve laboratory data management and visibility, establishment of national genomic sequencing capacity, implementation of Quality Management Systems and accreditation preparedness activities, and piloting of an integrated specimen referral model through collaboration with NIPOST in two states. While several of these initiatives remain works in progress, they have laid important foundations for a more connected, quality-assured, and resilient public health laboratory system. .
The laboratory pillar moved through three clear moments: emergency testing expansion, rapid diagnostic scale-up, and systems strengthening for sustainability.

This phase was characterised by the rapid expansion of COVID-19 testing capacity through the activation and scale-up of molecular testing laboratories across the country. NCDC leveraged existing GeneXpert platforms originally deployed for TB and HIV programmes to rapidly expand the laboratory network, enabling COVID-19 testing while maintaining essential diagnostic services. Emergency investments supported laboratory infrastructure upgrades, procurement of test kits and consumables, workforce training, biosafety improvements, and temporary specimen referral arrangements. While these interventions significantly increased testing capacity and geographic coverage, they also exposed critical system gaps, including limited subnational diagnostic coverage, fragmented specimen transport systems, and the absence of an integrated laboratory information system. These challenges informed the shift from emergency response to laboratory systems strengthening in Phase 2.

The second phase was the most investment-intensive period. It focused on decentralising diagnostics and building the systems required for a national laboratory network. A major intervention was deployment of 333 Truenat molecular testing units to health facilities across the country. In parallel, 370 portable digital X-ray units strengthened TB case finding and respiratory diagnostics. The phase also supported nationwide capacity building on molecular diagnostics, Ag-RDT testing, biosafety, quality assurance, laboratory data management and next-generation sequencing. Through technical assistance support to NCDC, 183 healthcare workers were trained on Ag-RDT implementation and integrated diagnostic approaches, while 74 laboratory personnel received specialised training on molecular diagnostic testing, helping expand workforce capacity for decentralised testing across the country. . eN-LIS implementation began, integrated zonal reference laboratory work was initiated, and the Luminex MAGPIX platform was deployed at NIMR. By the end of Phase 2, Nigeria had significantly expanded its diagnostic footprint and laid the foundation for a more integrated multi-disease laboratory system.

The third phase focused on sustainability and quality. Nineteen public health laboratories were upgraded. Integrated zonal reference laboratory development continued. Biomedical engineering capacity was strengthened through maintenance hubs, tools, spare parts and training. Genomic surveillance advanced through a national genomic sequencing strategy, governance structures and expanded sequencing capacity at NRL Gaduwa and CPHL. QMS and accreditation preparedness activities were implemented through standardized procedures, internal audits, document control and SLIPTA-aligned improvement processes. The National Integrated Specimen Referral Network pilot with NIPOST showed the feasibility of using government logistics infrastructure for structured and trackable specimen transport. By the end of Phase 3, the pillar had moved from emergency testing toward a broader laboratory systems-strengthening platform.

**Decentralised molecular diagnostics expanded.** A total of 333 Truenat molecular testing units were deployed to peripheral and secondary health facilities, improving access to molecular diagnostics in low-resource and hard-to-reach settings. **GeneXpert multi-disease use strengthened.** GeneXpert platforms were optimized for COVID-19, TB and HIV viral load testing, supported by approximately 2.7 million cartridges. **Portable diagnostic imaging expanded.** A total of 370 portable digital X-ray units were deployed to priority facilities, strengthening TB case finding and respiratory disease diagnostics. **Public health laboratory infrastructure upgraded.** Nineteen public health laboratories were upgraded and equipped, improving diagnostic readiness for outbreak detection, surveillance and response. **Genomic sequencing capacity strengthened.** Nigeria developed and approved a National Genomic Surveillance Strategy, established governance structures and expanded sequencing capacity at NRL Gaduwa and CPHL Lagos. **Central Public Health Laboratory strengthened.** CPHL was upgraded and equipped to support reference testing, genomic surveillance, outbreak investigation and emergency preparedness. **Laboratory workforce capacity expanded.** Nationwide training and refresher training strengthened capacity in molecular diagnostics, Ag-RDTs, biosafety and biosecurity, QMS, data management and integrated disease testing. Creation, printing and distribution of job aids on Tuberculosis Laboratory Services (TBLS) for capacity building of laboratory technicians. **Biomedical engineerin
**Quality systems advanced.** QMS implementation and accreditation preparedness activities moved national and subnational laboratories closer to verifiable quality standards.

The laboratory pillar’s strongest strategic value was the move from emergency COVID-19 testing toward a multi-disease diagnostic platform. GeneXpert systems were not treated as single-use COVID-19 assets. They were used to support COVID-19, TB and HIV viral load testing through shared infrastructure, workforce and supply chains. Truenat deployment extended the same principle to peripheral facilities, bringing molecular diagnostics closer to communities and underserved populations. Portable digital X-ray units strengthened TB and respiratory disease diagnosis, while Ag-RDTs expanded access to community and integrated febrile-illness testing. This multi-disease design protected the value of earlier TB and HIV investments while allowing Nigeria to respond to COVID-19. It also left the country with a broader diagnostic platform that can support surveillance, outbreak response and routine disease control beyond the pandemic.

**NACA and NTBLCP — grant and diagnostic deployment backbone.** NACA and NTBLCP supported grant management, procurement oversight, Truenat and GeneXpert deployment and infrastructure upgrades. **NCDC — technical leadership and public health laboratory strengthening.** NCDC led key technical components, including genomic sequencing, QMS, accreditation preparation, eN-LIS and public health laboratory systems. **State Ministries of Health — state implementation and service delivery.** States supported facility readiness, staffing, training participation and deployment of testing platforms. **Reference laboratories — operational anchors.** CPHL, NRL Gaduwa, NIMR, LUTH and zonal laboratories anchored reference testing, sequencing, QMS and advanced diagnostics. **NIPOST — specimen referral innovation.** NIPOST provided a government-based logistics platform for piloting structured specimen referral. **UNICEF ****—**** contribution for capacity building of laboratory technicians. **Printing and delivery of 30,000 pages job aids on Tuberculosis Laboratory Services (TBLS) for capacity building of laboratory technicians.

Together, these contributors helped Nigeria move from emergency COVID-19 testing to a broader diagnostic platform. The combined result was stronger molecular diagnostics, more decentralized testing, digital information systems, genomic sequencing capacity, specimen referral pilots, quality-management systems and a more visible laboratory network.

**Multi-disease platform design.** GeneXpert and Truenat investments were designed for COVID-19, TB, HIV and other priority diseases, protecting earlier investments and improving efficiency. **Tiered laboratory network strengthening.** Investments across reference, zonal, state and peripheral levels helped create a more coherent national diagnostic structure. **Biomedical engineering as a sustainability input.** BME hubs, tools and training helped address the maintenance gap that often weakens equipment-heavy investments. **Quality management as an institutional anchor.** QMS and accreditation preparedness helped move laboratories from emergency testing capacity toward verifiable quality standards. **Governance-first genomic sequencing.** Sequencing investments were paired with strategy and governance, helping ensure genomic data could feed surveillance and response. **Government-based specimen referral.** Using NIPOST for the NiSRN pilot tested a more sustainable alternative to parallel donor-funded specimen transport systems. **Digital laboratory information layer.** eN-LIS was designed as a network management tool, supporting ordering, tracking, results and quality indicators.

**Repeat:** Design every diagnostic platform for multi-disease use from the beginning. **Redesign:** Equipment deployment must be co-designed with reagent supply, forecasting and buffer stock systems. **Start earlier:** QMS should begin in the first phase of emergency laboratory investments, not near close-out. **Plan sustainability from inception:** Reagents, eN-LIS, BME hubs, sequencing and specimen referral need costed transition plans from the start. **Institutionalize BME capacity:** Maintenance should be a programme essential, not an optional add-on. **Govern data systems:** eN-LIS requires site-level data roles, management review and routine data-quality protocols. **Match technology to facility context:** Peripheral platforms should be selected based on real operating conditions, including power, space, staffing and connectivity.

C19RM’s laboratory investment created value far beyond COVID-19 testing. It expanded molecular testing access, strengthened multi-disease diagnostics, introduced domestic genomic sequencing capacity, initiated digital laboratory information systems, advanced QMS and accreditation preparation, strengthened BME capacity and piloted a government-linked specimen referral model. The deeper value is structural. Nigeria now has a stronger base for a tiered, multi-disease, digitally visible and quality-oriented laboratory network. This is a major preparedness dividend because laboratories are not only diagnostic assets; they are early-warning, surveillance and response infrastructure.

Nigeria’s Joint External Evaluation provides a strong lens for reading the laboratory pillar. In 2017, Nigeria had laboratory testing capacity for some priority diseases, but specimen referral scored 1, point-of-care and laboratory diagnostics scored 2, and laboratory quality scored 2. By 2023, laboratory quality, testing capacity and diagnostic network indicators had moved to 3, while specimen referral improved to 2. This should not be read as direct attribution, but it is a clear maturity signal. C19RM reinforced several areas the JEE identified as important: molecular testing, specimen referral, laboratory quality, diagnostic network expansion, BME capacity, interoperability and public health laboratory strengthening.

The JEE lens sharpens the laboratory story. Nigeria did not reach sustainable capacity across all laboratory functions, but the system moved from a fragmented, emergency-stretched base toward a more developed diagnostic network. C19RM helped reinforce that movement by investing in platforms, infrastructure, quality systems, digital reporting, specimen referral and workforce capacity. *Figure 33. Laboratory systems as a JEE maturity signal, 2017 → 2023 (comparable numeric areas).*

**Operational functionality was not fully visible.** There was no complete site-level dataset on what proportion of Truenat, GeneXpert, PDX, NGS and MAGPIX platforms were fully functional, actively used and operating near capacity. **Reagent continuity remained the central risk.** Equipment was deployed faster than long-term reagent financing and supply-chain continuity could be secured. Stockouts remained a major threat to operational capacity. **Cold-chain and reagent quality were not fully trackable.** Temperature integrity during last-mile distribution and facility storage remained unevenly documented. **eN-LIS adoption was inconsistent.** Connectivity, power, hardware limitations and user resistance affected consistent use of eN-LIS across sites. **QMS maturity varied by site.** Some laboratories advanced quickly, while others treated QMS as a compliance requirement rather than a routine quality culture. **Security and terrain limited supervision.** Conflict-affected and hard-to-reach states remained difficult to supervise consistently. **Human-resource retention remained fragile.** Highly trained laboratory professionals were vulnerable to migration, transfers and private-sector recruitment. **Federal-state governance gaps persisted.** State ownership, financing and operational support varied, affecting sustainability of federally supported laboratory investments.

**Protect reagent financing as the first sustainability priority.** The laboratory network will only remain useful if GeneXpert cartridges, Truenat chips, NGS reagents and other consumables are financed beyond the grant. **Make multi-disease testing the default.** GeneXpert, Truenat and PDX platforms should remain integrated across COVID-19, TB, HIV and other priority diseases. **Institutionalize BME hubs.** BME hubs need recurrent financing, staffing, transport, tools, spare parts and service protocols to protect the equipment portfolio. **Complete eN-LIS adoption.** eN-LIS should become the routine operating system for specimen tracking, result reporting, turnaround-time monitoring and network performance. **Move QMS from project activity to laboratory culture.** Accreditation preparation should continue, but the deeper goal is routine quality practice in every laboratory. **Expand specimen referral through government systems.** The NIPOST-linked NiSRN pilot should be assessed and, where feasible, expanded as a government-owned specimen transport model. **Retain the laboratory workforce.** Training gains will only last if laboratory scientists, data managers and biomedical engineers remain in public service roles with career pathways and incentives. **Treat laboratories as health-security infrastructure.** Laboratories are not just testing sites. They are surveillance, outbreak detection, AMR monitoring, genomic intelligence and emergency response infrastructure.
*Phase: Phase 3 — Systems Strengthening | Pillar Author: Maryam**↑ Back to Pillar Index*

Community Systems Strengthening was the part of C19RM that brought the response closer to households, wards, workplaces and facilities. COVID-19 showed that service continuity could not be protected only from national platforms. It also required trusted local structures that could identify barriers, monitor services, support referrals, raise concerns and mobilize practical solutions where people actually seek care. The CSS pillar responded by strengthening community-led systems and civil society networks as part of the health-response architecture. Its strongest results came where community structures were localized, linked to trusted CSOs and CBOs, and connected directly to Ward Development Committees, primary health care facilities, workplace channels and community-led monitoring teams. By engaging 105 Community-Based Organizations across seven implementing states, the project helped decentralize accountability to ward, PHC and community levels. It also reactivated and strengthened Ward Development Committees across 350 supported wards, giving communities a more structured role in health monitoring, local advocacy and service improvement. The pillar’s value was not only in community mobilization. It was in showing that communities, when equipped with data, tools and organized channels, can influence service quality, facility readiness, rights protection, workplace health access and local problem-solving. Examples from Nasarawa and Ondo showed how community evidence and advocacy could unlock local resources for water access, facility furniture and improved care conditions.
The CSS pillar moved through three clear moments: emergency community mobilization, rapid scale-up of community structures, and institutio`nalization of community accountability.

The first phase focused on rapid mobilization of national civil society networks, including NEPWHAN, the TB Network and ACOMIN. These networks supported community-level disease tracking, emergency awareness and service-continuity messaging during the acute phase of COVID-19. This phase helped maintain community touchpoints during a period of major service disruption. It also showed that communities were not passive recipients of information; they were essential actors in sustaining access, trust and continuity.

The second phase formalized and expanded community structures. Call centre volunteers were recruited, trained and engaged across Kwara, Ondo, Oyo, Plateau, Kaduna and Kano. GBV and IPV referral linkages were integrated through the NACA ATM Networks GBV Call Directory. Workplace programming through the National Labour Congress expanded public health sensitization, screening and referral. This phase helped convert community mobilization into more structured systems for monitoring, referral, legal literacy and workplace-based access.

The third phase embedded community accountability and monitoring into routine structures. The project formally engaged 105 CBOs across seven implementing states and trained 356 community representatives, programme officers and WDC representatives on CLM activities. Implementation reached peak field capacity across Akwa Ibom, Edo, Sokoto, Bauchi, Oyo, Ondo and Nasarawa. By 2025, 212 individuals had been trained on CLM data collection, teams transitioned into new target wards, and community evidence was used to drive advocacy and facility-level improvements. The phase closed with a focus on transferring community monitoring systems, scorecards and referral tools into more permanent national and subnational health structures.

**CBO implementation platform established.** A total of 105 Community-Based Organizations were engaged across twenty target states, with 15 CBOs per state anchoring community accountability and monitoring. **Community capacity built at scale.** The project trained 356 community representatives, programme officers and WDC representatives through virtual capacity-building sessions on CLM activities. **Ward Development Committees reactivated.** WDCs were restored and strengthened across 350 wards, helping align community structures with government sustainability guidance and local health intervention management. **Workplace channels expanded.** Workplace interventions reached 33,887 people with health information and referrals, showing the value of non-traditional service-delivery routes. **Workplace testing delivered.** Diagnostic testing was completed for 13,780 individuals, including 8,717 males and 5,063 females, through corporate and workplace screening channels. **Community evidence generated.** The project conducted 210 FGD sessions — 105 entry and 105 exit FGDs — across seven implementing states, creating a structured feedback loop for community issues. **Community issues documented.** A total of 555 community, service-delivery and human-rights issues were identified through FGD sessions and used to inform state advocacy. **Facility engagement exceeded targets.** The project completed 2,543 facility-based Community Engagement Interactions, assessing ATM service quality and human-rights concerns. **CLM reporting sustained.** All 105 Community-Led Monitoring Teams report

**Facility and governance improvements achieved.** Community advocacy contributed to skilled personnel deployment and facility improvements across a 15-state footprint, including Akwa Ibom, Anambra, Bauchi, Delta, Edo, Gombe, Jigawa, Kano, Kwara, Nasarawa, Ogun, Ondo, Oyo, Plateau and Rivers. *Figure 34. Community systems field footprint across seven implementing states.*

t: 4. Spotlight Achievement: Community Evidence to Local Action
x: The CSS pillar showed its strongest value where community evidence translated into visible facility improvement. In Sabon Gari community, Doma LGA, Nasarawa State, the local CBO, Child Education and Community Development Initiative, identified critical water and IPC gaps at PHC Alwaza. Advocacy to local stakeholders led a community philanthropist to fund a ₦465,000 water intervention, including procurement and installation of a new submersible borehole pump linked to an overhead storage tank. This restored running water for maternal and newborn care. In Imafon, Akure North LGA, Ondo State, the Handicapped Development Intervention Center used CLM focus groups at Imafon Basic Health Centre to document the lack of basic amenities, including unprotected and flattened mattresses. The CLMT escalated the findings to the Olu of Ala and his council of chiefs. Community leadership mobilized ₦326,000 to procure protected mattresses and facility furniture. These examples show the practical value of community systems: local data, local advocacy and local leadership can correct problems that formal systems may miss or delay. *Figure 35. Community evidence unlocking local capital for facility fixes (₦).*

The workplace component extended the reach of CSS beyond health facilities, reaching 33,887 people and testing 13,780 individuals through corporate and labour structures. **NACA — coordination and accountability.** NACA coordinated the CSS investment, verified reports, oversaw fund management and enforced accountability, including the suspension and replacement of civil society groups where misconduct was identified. **ATM Networks — national-to-community bridge.** NEPWHAN, the TB Network and ACOMIN translated national grant priorities into community monitoring, training, advocacy and quarterly reporting. **CBOs and CLMTs — local implementation and feedback.** The 105 CBOs and CLMTs generated community evidence, conducted facility interactions, documented barriers and moved local issues into advocacy channels. **Ward Development Committees — local ownership.** WDCs helped anchor community action at ward level, connecting PHC facilities, community leaders and local health priorities. **NLC — workplace health access.** The National Labour Congress helped embed public health sensitization, testing and referral into workplace settings, extending the reach of community systems.

Together, these contributors helped community systems move beyond emergency mobilization. NACA provided coordination and accountability. ATM networks gave the work national community reach. CBOs and CLMTs generated local evidence. WDCs anchored ward-level ownership. Workplace partners extended service access. The combined result was a stronger community accountability platform for service continuity, rights protection and local problem-solving.

**Zero-tolerance accountability.** Swift suspension and replacement of non-compliant CSOs protected programme integrity and grant resources. **Ward-level institutional anchoring.** Reactivating WDCs helped move community systems from short-term mobilization toward routine PHC-linked accountability. **Harmonized grant streams.** Aligning CBO allowances, FGDs and data collation across C19RM and RSSH helped reduce duplication and fragmentation. **Virtual capacity building.** Virtual training allowed hundreds of community actors to build skills despite geography and logistics constraints. **Workplace delivery routes.** Partnering with labour and corporate structures created new access points for health education, screening and referral. **Paired entry-exit monitoring.** Entry and exit FGDs, combined with facility interactions, created a stronger feedback loop for service barriers and rights concerns.

**Repeat:** Continue using CSO/CBO data systems to identify barriers, document human-rights issues and drive local advocacy. **Redesign:** Replace slow manual reporting with real-time digital dashboards that give national and state teams clearer visibility. **Institutionalize:** Community evidence, scorecards and referral tools should be embedded within state and national health governance structures. **Strengthen:** Digital literacy and connectivity support must be built into community monitoring systems. **Protect:** CLM allowances and platform costs require predictable financing; otherwise, community reporting systems will weaken quickly.

C19RM helped bring community systems into Nigeria’s formal health-response architecture in a more structured way. It positioned CLM as a practical accountability tool, strengthened national CSO networks as government partners, reactivated ward-level structures and validated workplace channels as routes for public health delivery. The deeper value was the bridge it created between community experience and decision-making. Through CBOs, WDCs, CLMTs, workplace partners and advocacy structures, community issues became visible, documented and actionable.

Community Systems Strengthening does not map to a single JEE technical area, but it connects strongly to several 2023 JEE priorities: community engagement, service continuity, subnational coordination, workforce, RCCE and health-services provision. The 2023 JEE emphasized the need to bridge gaps between authorities, service providers and communities, and to strengthen whole-of-society engagement. This should not be read as direct attribution. It is better understood as a preparedness contribution. C19RM helped strengthen community-level systems in areas that matter for IHR implementation: service continuity, local feedback, community trust, referral linkages, rights protection and subnational accountability.

The maturity signal is clear: C19RM did not create community systems from scratch, but it helped move them from emergency mobilization toward a more routine accountability layer for service continuity and preparedness.

**Digital systems were vulnerable to administrative disruption.** The EIMS platform became non-functional because of delays in cloud hosting and domain renewals, forcing a return to manual tracking. **Subnational digital capacity remained uneven.** Some rural cadres lacked the digital literacy, devices or connectivity needed to fully use virtual training and reporting systems. **Community systems depended heavily on donor financing.** State and local health departments lacked independent budget lines to sustain CLM after the grant period. **Administrative delays affected grassroots morale.** Delayed fund disbursements and monthly allowances affected CLMT motivation and field continuity. **Manual reporting weakened timeliness.** Where digital platforms failed, manual reporting reduced speed, consistency and real-time visibility. **Hard-to-reach communities remained difficult to monitor.** Remote and marginalized populations required more time, funding and field support than standard implementation timelines allowed.

**Make CLM part of routine health accountability.** Community-led monitoring should not remain grant-dependent. It should become part of routine state and PHC accountability systems. **Finance community systems domestically.** CBO allowances, digital platforms, data collection and advocacy meetings need state and domestic budget support. **Keep WDCs active beyond the grant.** The 350-ward footprint should be used for routine PHC monitoring, community feedback and service-improvement advocacy. **Protect the digital backbone.** Hosting, domain renewal, EIMS functionality and dashboard maintenance should be financed as basic operating costs. **Strengthen community data use.** Community evidence should feed into state review meetings, PHC plans and facility improvement actions. **Use workplaces as routine service channels.** The NLC workplace model should continue as a route for health education, testing, referral and service linkage. **Treat community trust as preparedness infrastructure.** Preparedness depends not only on facilities and commodities. It also depends on communities that trust the system, report problems early and help keep services working.

ATM mitigation was the part of C19RM that protected Nigeria’s HIV, TB and malaria platforms from becoming secondary casualties of the pandemic. COVID-19 did not create the risks to HIV, TB and malaria services; it exposed how quickly movement restrictions, fear of facility attendance, disrupted outreach, commodity delays, strained laboratories and economic pressure on clients could weaken disease-control gains built over many years. The risk was practical and immediate. PLHIV still needed HIV testing, ART refills, adherence support, viral load services, EID services, PrEP refills, condoms, HIV self-test kits and linkage to care. People with presumptive TB still needed access to molecular diagnosis, drug-resistant TB testing and rapid treatment linkage. Malaria programmes still needed surveys, LLIN campaigns, SMC delivery, commodities, field supervision and community-level delivery to continue under COVID-safe conditions. The HIV mitigation evidence was particularly clear. A national data review before implementation showed a linkage gap of 123,230, with 1,492,152 of 1,615,382 people linked to treatment — about 93% — and viral load coverage of 68%. A deeper 2020 programme analysis across Global Fund-supported sites in 13 states showed suboptimal retention at 83%, PrEP continuation at 61%, and weaker viral suppression among children and adolescents living with HIV: 57.7% among children and 76.5% among adolescents. COVID-19 made these gaps sharper. Clients avoided facilities and OSS because of fear of infection, reduced income limited transport to care, health workers were aff

C19RM responded by financing the practical adjustments needed to keep ATM services moving under pressure. For HIV, this meant community outreach teams, CBO support, community pharmacies, Community ART Management teams, home delivery of ARVs, MMD 3–6, virtual case management, telemedicine, ECHO platforms, situation rooms, CALHIV caregiver forums, COVID screening and vaccination messaging for PLHIV, and IPC materials for HIV service settings. For TB, it meant protecting and strengthening GeneXpert laboratories through solar power, air conditioning, cold chain equipment, grounding systems, surge protection, training for 72 new GeneXpert facilities and cartridge procurement. For malaria, it meant COVID-safe implementation of surveys and campaigns, ICT4D, BYOD, digital registration and field accountability. The defining value of the ATM pillar was therefore not one disease-specific activity. It was the deliberate effort to protect the operating systems carrying HIV, TB and malaria during a national shock. C19RM helped move service continuity from a set of emergency workarounds into a more structured adaptation model: community delivery, digital coordination, decentralized refills, diagnostic continuity, campaign digitization and stronger field monitoring. The pillar’s centre of gravity sat in emergency mitigation and rapid scale-up, but its forward legacy is broader. It showed that major disease programmes can continue during emergencies when services are diversified, communities are mobilized, diagnostics are protected, digital platforms are used, and programme management can c
The ATM mitigation pillar moved through three clear moments: emergency protection of services, rapid adaptation of programme delivery, and early institutionalization of service-continuity practices.

The first phase was about preventing service collapse. COVID-19 restrictions, facility avoidance and pressure on health workers created immediate risks for HIV, TB and malaria services. For HIV, COVID-19 heightened fear of facility attendance and disrupted access to prevention, testing, treatment and monitoring services. For TB, diagnostic systems faced pressure as molecular platforms and laboratory staff were drawn into COVID-19 testing. For malaria, survey and campaign delivery had to be adapted to reduce infection risk while preserving field reach. This phase was mainly protective. It helped keep programmes functioning while the country adjusted to a new operating environment.

The second phase converted emergency mitigation into structured programme adjustments. FHI360 implemented C19RM HIV mitigation activities as a Sub-Recipient to NACA across 13 Global Fund-supported states: Anambra, Ebonyi, Taraba, Abia, Edo, Imo, Enugu, Plateau, Kwara, Kaduna, Kano, Gombe and Oyo. AHNI, ECEWS and SFH implemented as sub-sub-recipients in their respective states, building on existing HIV grant structures to reduce operational duplication. HIV activities focused on improving access to COVID screening and vaccination among PLHIV, finalizing adverse-event monitoring tools, developing IPC materials in the context of HIV and COVID-19, engaging community outreach teams, supporting CBOs, expanding differentiated ART service delivery, using community pharmacies for PrEP and ART refills, and establishing virtual platforms for adherence counselling, telemedicine, appointment booking and supervision. For TB, IHVN implemented the GeneXpert Laboratory Optimization Project under NACA oversight and in collaboration with NTBLCP, State TB Programmes, facility management teams and vendors. The work addressed infrastructure and operational risks affecting molecular TB diagnostics, including unstable power, poor environmental conditions, weak cold chain, limited workforce capacity and diagnostic commodity disruptions. For malaria, the rapid scale-up phase supported COVID-safe campaign implementation, digitization of LLIN and SMC delivery, ICT4D, BYOD approaches and field accountability mechanisms.

The third phase focused on what could remain useful beyond the acute pandemic period. For HIV, virtual case management, community ART refill structures, community pharmacies, situation rooms and ECHO platforms offered a more flexible model for continuity of care. For TB, optimized GeneXpert sites, trained personnel, improved power systems, cold chain equipment and cartridge continuity strengthened the durability of molecular diagnostic services. For malaria, ICT4D and BYOD approaches demonstrated more efficient ways to support large-scale field campaigns and beneficiary registration. This phase showed that ATM mitigation was not only about protecting services during COVID-19. It generated practical lessons on how essential disease programmes can remain operational during future emergencies.

**HIV service-continuity gap clearly diagnosed.** The HIV mitigation work was grounded in data. Pre-implementation analysis identified a national linkage gap of 123,230, viral load coverage of 68%, 83% retention on ART across GF-supported sites, 61% PrEP continuation among high-risk groups, and weaker viral suppression among children and adolescents. This gave the HIV mitigation package a clear programmatic rationale. **Community HIV delivery expanded across 13 states.** C19RM supported additional Community Outreach Teams and CBO-linked community facilitators across Anambra, Ebonyi, Taraba, Imo, Enugu, Edo, Oyo, Kano, Kaduna, Abia, Kwara, Plateau and Gombe. The community model was designed to sustain HIV case finding, prevention services, condom and lubricant distribution, PrEP refills, HIVST access, linkage and retention during COVID-related restrictions. **Differentiated ART delivery strengthened.** The pillar expanded MMD 3–6, community and home ARV delivery, drug pick-up centres and community pharmacy models to reduce unnecessary facility visits and protect ART continuity. Anambra received support for additional community drug pick-up centres, while home delivery was supported across all 13 states. **Community pharmacies and CAM teams used for retention.** Community pharmacies, Community ART Management teams and peer support groups were engaged to track clients on ART and PrEP, provide reminders, support follow-up, manage missed appointments and strengthen retention during the pandemic period. **Virtual case management and telemedicine introduced.** Virtual case manager

**ECHO and situation rooms supported HIV coordination.** ECHO video conferencing centres were supported in Anambra and Ebonyi to strengthen monitoring, supervision, peer learning and performance review. Ebonyi’s situation room helped improve real-time data visualization, site-level monitoring, LGA-specific review and course correction for HIV and PMTCT programming. **CALHIV caregiver support adapted to COVID realities.** A virtual caregiver forum was supported in Anambra and Ebonyi to improve adherence, ARV uptake, counselling, health education and U=U messaging for children and adolescents living with HIV. **COVID protection integrated for PLHIV and HIV service teams.** Tools and materials were developed for adverse-event monitoring, COVID screening and vaccination messaging, and IPC in the context of COVID and HIV. Five thousand copies of monitoring and reporting tools were planned for use among healthcare providers across the four states of Anambra, Abia, Ebonyi and Taraba. **GeneXpert laboratories optimized nationwide.** IHVN’s GeneXpert Laboratory Optimization Project optimized and certified 396 GeneXpert laboratories across all 36 states and the FCT. This strengthened the operational environment for molecular TB diagnosis during a period of pandemic disruption. **Power, environmental and cold-chain conditions improved for TB diagnostics.** Optimization packages included solar power systems, inverters, batteries, combiner boxes, air conditioning, refrigerator/freezers, grounding systems, lightning arrestors, surge protection and electrical distribution systems. These i

**TB cartridge continuity protected.** The project supported procurement, warehousing, insurance, quality assurance, storage and nationwide distribution of GeneXpert cartridges for routine TB and drug-resistant TB testing, helping reduce the risk of diagnostic interruption during global commodity instability. **Malaria field continuity and campaign digitization supported.** C19RM supported malaria programme adaptation through COVID-safe campaign implementation, ICT4D, digital field tools and BYOD approaches for LLIN and SMC activities. *Figure 37. HIV service-continuity baseline that shaped the mitigation package.*

t: 4. Spotlight Achievement: Keeping HIV Care Close to Clients
x: One of the strongest examples of ATM mitigation was the HIV continuity model implemented through FHI360 and its sub-sub-recipients. The model recognized that during COVID-19, facility avoidance was not simply a behavioural problem. It was a rational response to fear of infection, transport constraints, economic hardship, longer waiting times and reduced provider availability. C19RM therefore supported a more flexible delivery model. Community Outreach Teams were engaged to intensify HIV testing and prevention services. CBOs were supported to deliver prevention products, including condoms, lubricants, PrEP refills and HIV self-test kits. Community pharmacies and other differentiated drug distribution platforms were used to provide ART and PrEP refills closer to clients. CAM teams and peer support groups tracked clients, provided reminders and supported retention. Virtual case managers and adherence counsellors provided counselling, risk assessment, linkage support, adverse drug effect follow-up and appointment booking. The design was practical. It brought services closer to clients, reduced unnecessary facility visits, protected health workers and clients from COVID exposure, and preserved HIV treatment continuity in 13 Global Fund-supported states. The model did not remove all gaps, but it gave Nigeria an important service-continuity template for future emergencies: keep clients close to care even when facilities are under pressure.

t: 5. Spotlight Achievement: GeneXpert Optimization as TB Continuity Infrastructure
x: The IHVN GeneXpert Laboratory Optimization Project was one of the clearest TB service-continuity investments under the ATM pillar. The intervention recognized that molecular diagnostics depend on much more than equipment. GeneXpert machines require stable power, controlled temperature, cold-chain support for cartridges and specimens, trained personnel, maintenance systems and reliable commodity supply. The project optimized 396 GeneXpert laboratories nationwide. Solar power systems reduced dependence on unstable electricity supply and generators. Air conditioning improved the operating environment for equipment. Refrigerator/freezers strengthened reagent and specimen storage. Grounding, lightning protection and surge devices reduced equipment damage risk. Facility-based training for 72 new GeneXpert facilities strengthened local competence in testing, QA, biosafety and troubleshooting. This investment protected TB molecular testing at a time when COVID-19 could have diverted platforms, staff and commodities. More importantly, it left behind infrastructure that can serve the TB programme beyond the pandemic: better-powered laboratories, better-protected equipment, stronger cold-chain conditions, trained personnel and improved diagnostic resilience. *Figure 38. GeneXpert optimisation as TB diagnostic continuity infrastructure.*

Together, these contributors helped protect ATM services during a period of major disruption. HIV services moved closer to communities and digital platforms. TB diagnostics became more resilient through optimized laboratories, trained staff and cartridge continuity. Malaria campaigns adopted digital and COVID-safe field methods. The combined result was a more resilient disease-control operating model under emergency conditions.

**Community-first HIV continuity.** The use of COTs, CBOs, community pharmacies, CAM teams and peer support groups helped sustain HIV testing, prevention, treatment refills, PrEP continuation and client tracking when facility access was constrained. **Differentiated ART delivery under emergency conditions.** MMD, home delivery and community pick-up models reduced unnecessary facility visits while protecting ART continuity. **Virtual case management and supervision.** Telemedicine, virtual adherence counselling, call centres, ECHO platforms and situation rooms helped preserve client follow-up and programme oversight despite movement restrictions. **Data-informed HIV programme management.** Situation rooms allowed programme data from multiple sources to support frequent review, site performance monitoring and course correction. **GeneXpert optimization as a systems package.** IHVN’s work showed that diagnostics require infrastructure, power, environmental control, cold chain, training, QA and commodities — not equipment alone. **Solar power for diagnostic resilience.** Renewable energy investments helped reduce interruptions linked to unstable electricity and generator dependency. **Facility-based training and mentorship.** Training staff in their operational environment helped strengthen practical competence, troubleshooting, biosafety and routine maintenance. **Campaign digitization for malaria continuity.** ICT4D and BYOD approaches improved field visibility, campaign tracking and accountability during large-scale malaria interventions.

**Repeat:** Build community delivery into emergency plans for HIV, TB and malaria from the start. Community models should not be improvised after service disruption begins. **Redesign:** Disease programmes should track continuity indicators against pre-emergency baselines, so the effect of mitigation can be measured more clearly. **Institutionalize:** Virtual case management, ECHO, situation rooms and telemedicine should remain part of routine HIV programme management. **Protect diagnostics as an operating system.** GeneXpert continuity depends on power, temperature, cold chain, trained users, maintenance and cartridges. Future TB investments should fund these as a complete package. **Budget for supervision and verification.** Large-scale infrastructure optimization needs post-installation assessment, vendor follow-up and certification built into the budget from the beginning. **Sustain community pharmacy models.** Community pharmacies can support PrEP and ART refills, client tracking and retention if agreements, incentives, data flows and quality supervision are maintained. **Keep malaria campaigns digitally visible.** Digital field tools should remain part of campaign design because they improve accountability and allow quicker correction during implementation.

C19RM helped Nigeria protect its major disease-control platforms during one of the most disruptive public health events in recent history. The ATM pillar’s value was not only that specific activities were implemented. Its deeper value was that it helped HIV, TB and malaria programmes adapt quickly enough to keep services moving. For HIV, the pillar strengthened community delivery, differentiated ART access, PrEP and prevention continuity, virtual case management, PLHIV COVID protection and digital supervision. For TB, it protected molecular diagnostic continuity by optimizing 396 GeneXpert laboratories, training 72 new sites and sustaining cartridge availability. For malaria, it supported COVID-safe campaign implementation and digitized field accountability. The lasting value is the continuity model it helped demonstrate: essential disease programmes need community reach, diagnostic resilience, digital management, campaign adaptation and state-level monitoring to withstand future shocks.

Nigeria’s Joint External Evaluation provides a useful health-security lens for reading the ATM mitigation pillar. ATM mitigation does not map to a single JEE technical area, but it directly reinforces several IHR capacities: continuity of essential health services, case management, emergency logistics and supply chain, laboratory systems, workforce, surveillance and service utilization. This should not be read as direct attribution. It is better understood as a maturity signal. COVID-19 exposed how quickly essential services could be disrupted, while C19RM helped reinforce the systems that allowed HIV, TB and malaria services to continue under pressure.
