The JEE lens sharpens the ATM story. The pillar did not solve Nigeria’s service-continuity challenge. But it helped demonstrate how essential HIV, TB and malaria services can be protected when community systems, diagnostics, logistics, digital tools and disease programme leadership are brought together during a crisis. *Figure 39. ATM mitigation as a JEE maturity signal, 2017 → 2023 (comparable numeric areas).*

**Service-continuity outcomes were not fully quantified.** The pillar documents many adaptations, but evidence remains limited on how strongly they preserved HIV, TB and malaria service volumes against pre-pandemic baselines. **Disease-specific reporting remained fragmented.** HIV, TB and malaria mitigation activities were reported through different implementers and programme structures, making integrated ATM-wide performance analysis difficult. **HIV performance was mixed across indicators.** The FHI360 combined scorecard showed strong performance in several KP and HIV testing indicators, including several indicators achieving 120%, and an overall FHI360 rating of 90.9% B. However, weaker performance was recorded in areas such as AGYW prevention, PMTCT ART coverage and some treatment-related indicators, showing that mitigation did not eliminate all service-continuity gaps. **Community delivery required stronger supervision.** Community teams, pharmacies and lay workers expanded service reach, but quality assurance, supervision, tracking and routine monitoring required sustained staffing and structured oversight. **Virtual models depended on connectivity and client access.** Telemedicine, virtual adherence counselling, caregiver forums and ECHO platforms depended on phones, data, connectivity, provider availability and client digital access. **Diagnostic continuity remained commodity-dependent.** GeneXpert optimization strengthened the operating environment, but TB testing continuity still depended on cartridge availability, procurement lead times, warehousing and distribut

**Final financial reconciliation remained necessary.** Available reports show rich implementation detail, but final line-item reconciliation across HIV, TB and malaria components should be completed before publication.

**Make service continuity a standing disease-programme function.** HIV, TB and malaria programmes should not wait for the next emergency to design continuity measures. Each programme needs pre-agreed models for community delivery, diagnostics, refills, referrals, campaigns and data reporting. **Keep community-based HIV delivery protected.** Community Outreach Teams, CBOs, community pharmacies, CAM teams, home delivery, MMD and community pick-up centres should remain part of the HIV continuity toolkit. **Institutionalize virtual HIV support.** Virtual case management, adherence counselling, appointment booking, telemedicine, ECHO and situation rooms should remain available for routine supervision, client follow-up and emergency continuity. **Protect CALHIV and caregiver support models.** Virtual caregiver forums for children and adolescents living with HIV should be sustained where they improve adherence, counselling and caregiver confidence. **Sustain GeneXpert optimization gains.** The 396 optimized GeneXpert laboratories need preventive maintenance, solar-system servicing, cold-chain monitoring, environmental control and equipment protection. **Secure cartridge financing and supply continuity.** GeneXpert cartridges for TB and DR-TB testing must remain financed and available. Without commodities, optimized laboratories cannot deliver diagnostic continuity. **Keep TB diagnostic resilience linked to national programme management.** NTBLCP, state TB programmes and facility teams should continue tracking functionality, test volume, downtime, cartridge availability and mainten

**Treat ATM platforms as preparedness infrastructure.** HIV, TB, and malaria programs are not separate from pandemic preparedness. They are part of the infrastructure that keeps essential services alive during shocks. Sustaining the gains means protecting the platforms, people, commodities, diagnostics and community systems that carry them.

*Phase: Phase 3 — Systems Strengthening | Pillar Author: Dr. Samiyah **&** Grace Ihundu*[*↑ Back to Pillar Index*](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ww7TLIT8yB9QrCwqV29-sG7T6LvjEg3kzbm4wkvitV4/edit#bookmark=id.hidbd6orbi0p)

*From access to continuously improved care.* The Quality Improvement (QI) pillar was implemented as a strategic health systems strengthening intervention designed to improve the quality, safety, efficiency, and continuity of healthcare services across supported facilities and communities in Nigeria. The intervention was developed in response to persistent quality-of-care gaps across HIV, tuberculosis (TB), malaria, COVID-19, maternal, newborn and child health (MNCH), and broader primary healthcare (PHC) services. A landmark 2018 Lancet Commission on High-Quality Health Systems in Low- and Middle-Income Countries found that clients receive less than half of the recommended evidence-based clinical interventions during routine healthcare visits, resulting in more deaths attributable to poor-quality care than to lack of access to care itself. These findings highlighted the need to move beyond increasing access to health services and focus on strengthening the quality and effectiveness of healthcare delivery. The intervention was initially implemented under the Global Fund Strategic Initiative for Service Delivery Innovation (SDI-SI), a multi-country initiative implemented across five countries in West and Central Africa through the RSSH. In Nigeria, the pilot was implemented in Jigawa State across five Local Government Areas (LGAs), where Jhpiego provided technical assistance to strengthen service delivery and institutionalize quality improvement approaches within health facilities and government systems. Following demonstrated improvements in patient outcomes, service delivery

The intervention adopted a government-led and systems-oriented approach grounded in the WHO Quality Planning Framework, the WHO Point of Care Quality Improvement (POCQI) methodology, and broader health systems strengthening principles. The approach supported healthcare workers, managers, supervisors, and community structures to identify service delivery gaps, conduct root cause analyses, test context-specific change ideas, and monitor performance using routine data and continuous learning cycles. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on classroom training, the intervention emphasized practical onsite mentorship, supportive supervision, peer-to-peer learning, collaborative problem-solving, and routine performance reviews to drive sustainable improvements in quality of care. Implementation focused on strengthening integrated service delivery across HIV, TB, malaria, COVID-19, MNCH, community health, and other PHC services. Facility Quality Improvement Teams (QITs) were established and strengthened to lead continuous improvement efforts, while Community Health Influencers, Promoters, and Services (CHIPs) and other community structures were engaged to strengthen referrals, treatment adherence, demand generation, continuity of care, and community-facility linkages. Beyond service delivery improvements, the intervention prioritized strengthening the foundational building blocks required for sustainable quality healthcare. This included strengthening governance and leadership systems, workforce capacity, health information systems, accountability mechanisms, and communi

The project further supported institutionalization of QI through integration into routine government structures, including onsite supportive supervision systems, peer-learning platforms, routine data review processes, digital reporting mechanisms, and government-led coordination structures. By embedding QI within existing health system processes rather than creating parallel systems, the intervention strengthened government ownership, health system resilience, and sustainability of service delivery improvements. Through this integrated health systems strengthening approach, the QI intervention contributed to measurable improvements in patient outcomes, service delivery performance, workforce competence, community engagement, and government ownership while demonstrating a scalable and sustainable model for improving quality of care within Nigeria's health system.

*A pilot-to-scale service delivery and improvement pathway* The QI intervention did not follow the C19RM phases in a linear way; its evolution is best understood as a pilot-to-scale across two intertwined dimensions. **Service Delivery Improvement:** QI methodologies were used to identify service delivery bottlenecks, implement improvement cycles, strengthen supportive supervision, and improve outcomes across HIV, TB, malaria, MNCH, community health, and PHC services. **Health Systems Strengthening:** The intervention strengthened governance, leadership, workforce capacity, routine data use, supportive supervision systems, community structures, and institutionalization of QI within government systems to promote sustainability.
*Translating workforce capacity to service outcomes.*

**• **Trained 90 state-level stakeholders, 251 LGA representatives, and 2,844 healthcare workers on integrated Quality Improvement (QI), leadership, and supportive supervision approaches. **• **Strengthened community systems through capacity building of 1,050 community health workers on Quality improvement for referral systems, reporting, and continuity of care. **• **Conducted 10,704 onsite supportive supervision (OSS) visits across supported facilities and mentored over 3,147 healthcare workers on implementation of QI cycles and service delivery improvement. **• **Strengthened collaborative learning through peer-to-peer learning platforms involving 954 healthcare workers and 985 community health workers. **• **Conducted 308 Supervisors Support Meetings (SSMs) to strengthen supportive supervision, accountability, and problem-solving systems across LGAs and facilities. Improved provider competency levels, with clinical knowledge scores of 94% in malaria, 91% in TB, and 88% in MNCH services from program evaluation.

**• **Integrated QI into pre-service curricula across 15 health training institutions, with 127 lecturers and tutors trained to sustain workforce development. **• **Advanced QI institutionalization across Ekiti, Gombe, and Jigawa States, with QI maturity scores improving from 2.4 to between 3.2 and 3.6 out of 4 points, reflecting progression from “emerging” to “maturing” QI systems. **• **Strengthened state governance and accountability systems through operational plans, budget lines, routine peer-learning forums, and integration of QI coaching into Integrated Supportive Supervision (ISS) systems. **• **Institutionalized Facility Quality Improvement Team (QIT) meetings, Supervisors Support Meetings (SSMs) into existing LGA Management structures, and integration of Ward Development Committees (WDCs) and community structures into QI implementation. **• **Supported government-led scale-up of QI implementation to 317 additional facilities through existing government systems and structures. **• **Mobilized over ₦46 million (USD 32,857) through government structures to support sustainability of QI activities. **• **Strengthened use of QI dashboards and routine data systems for performance monitoring and decision-making with government procuring over 400 dashboards for monitoring improvement cycles in the facilities

t: 4. Spotlight Achievement: From Pilot to State-Owned QI Scale-Up
x: *QI demonstrating technical support toward government-owned improvement practice.* One of the strongest achievements of the Quality Improvement pillar was its movement from a focused pilot into a state-owned service-delivery improvement model. The intervention began in Jigawa State as a Quality Improvement and Leadership & Management approach, then expanded through C19RM savings to Ekiti, Gombe and Jigawa. This expansion did not simply add more facilities. It demonstrated that QI could be scaled through government systems, LGA structures, facility teams, community actors and routine data-use platforms. The scale of the expansion is important. Across the three states, the intervention reached 44 LGAs and 821 health facilities, established and strengthened 865 Facility Quality Improvement Teams, and supported 3,913 improvement cycles. These cycles gave facility teams a practical method for identifying service gaps, testing change ideas, reviewing results and adjusting care processes using routine data. This moved QI away from being a one-off training activity and closer to a continuous management discipline inside facilities.

t: 4. Spotlight Achievement: From Pilot to State-Owned QI Scale-Up
x: The pillar also showed that state ownership could be built when QI was connected to operational plans, budget lines, dashboards, supervision systems and government-led review structures. Government-led scale-up to 317 additional facilities and the mobilization of over ₦46 million, equivalent to approximately US$32,857 demonstrated that QI had begun to move beyond donor-supported implementation into state-led continuation. The improvement in QI maturity scores from 2.4 to between 3.2 and 3.6 out of 4 points across Ekiti, Gombe and Jigawa was therefore more than a technical score. It signalled a shift from an emerging QI system toward a more mature, state-owned improvement platform. This spotlight matters because it captures the real contribution of the pillar: C19RM helped convert QI from a pilot and training model into a practical approach for improving integrated HIV, TB, malaria, MNCH, COVID-19 and PHC services through local problem-solving, facility ownership, community linkage and routine performance review. *Figure 42. *Additional system capability created by the QI * The investment shifted Quality Improvement (QI) from isolated training and supervision activities to a continuous, data-driven health systems strengthening approach embedded within routine government service delivery systems. This included establishment of QI focal persons at state and LGA levels, integration of QI into routine supportive supervision systems, state operational plans, budget lines, peer-learning platforms, pre-service curricula, and routine government coordination mechanisms across supported states. *Quality as a systems catalyst * Quality Improvement does not sit neatly inside one emergency-preparedness indicator, but it strengthens several components that make health systems more resilient: competent teams, routine data use, supportive supervision, integrated service delivery, local problem-solving, leadership accountability and community trust. The maturity signal is not only that facilities implemented improvement cycles. It is that states began to absorb QI into budgets, supervision systems, dashboards, pre-service curricula and management routines.

The QI maturity story is therefore a systems story. The intervention did not only train people or support supervision visits. It helped build a practical operating model for safer, more reliable and more integrated care. It strengthened the ability of facilities and states to see gaps, solve them, learn from them and sustain improvement through routine systems.

*What still needed to be enhanced and then scaled * Multiple parallel reporting platforms across disease programs created challenges in harmonizing data systems and integrating review actions across different levels of implementation. Weak integration of quality indicators into routine performance management systems. Limited adoption of a comprehensive national policy guideline for integrated Quality Improvement constrained standardization and broader institutionalization of QI approaches across programs and states. Frequent transfer and attrition of trained healthcare workers and government officials disrupted continuity of QI implementation, weakened institutional memory, and increased the need for repeated orientation and capacity building. Commodity stockouts and irregular supply chain systems affected adherence to clinical standards and continuity of quality service delivery across some facilities. High workload and limited human resources at facility level constrained regular conduct of QI meetings, documentation, mentorship activities, and implementation of improvement initiatives. Delayed or inconsistent remuneration for community health workers affected motivation, engagement, and continuity of community-level interventions. Release of budgeted government funds limited implementation of planned QI scale up activities at state and LGA levels. Variability in leadership commitment across national, state, and LGA levels affected ownership, coordination, accountability, and sustainability of QI implementation. Linkages between QI dashboards and routine state-level decis

Parallel data systems limited integrated performance management. Multiple disease-programme reporting platforms made it difficult to harmonize review actions across HIV, TB, malaria, MNCH, community health and PHC services. This limited the full value of QI as an integrated service-delivery improvement model. Quality indicators were not yet fully embedded in routine management. QI generated important facility and service-level insights, but quality indicators still needed stronger integration into routine government performance management, state review meetings and budget decision processes. National standardization required stronger policy anchoring. While the intervention aligned with Nigeria’s quality agenda, broader institutionalization would be strengthened by clearer national guidance for integrated QI implementation across programmes, states and service levels. Workforce mobility weakened continuity of improvement practice. Frequent transfers and attrition of trained healthcare workers and government officials disrupted continuity, weakened institutional memory and increased the need for repeated onboarding, mentorship and refresher training. Supply-chain gaps constrained adherence to care standards. Commodity stockouts and irregular supply-chain systems affected the ability of some facilities to fully apply clinical standards and sustain quality service delivery. QI can identify bottlenecks, but supply-chain reliability is required to solve many of them. Facility workload affected QI meeting discipline and documentation. High workload and limited human resources con

Community health workers and CHIPs structures played an important role in referral, follow-up and continuity of care, but delayed or inconsistent remuneration affected motivation and continuity. Sustaining community-linked QI will require predictable support and recognition systems. State commitment was visible, but financing execution remained uneven. The mobilization of state resources was a major gain, but release and execution of budgeted funds still varied. Future scale-up will depend on moving from budget inclusion to actual disbursement and routine financing. Dashboard visibility had not yet fully translated into management action. Dashboards improved visibility, but the next stage is routine use. QI dashboards must be linked to state and LGA management decisions, supervision priorities, budget discussions and corrective action.

*From donor-supported improvement to routine state management.* Building on the significant improvements achieved in patient outcomes, service delivery quality, and health system performance across Ekiti, Gombe, and Jigawa States, there is a strong opportunity to expand the integrated Quality Improvement (QI) model to additional states to promote greater equity, coverage, and health system impact. The intervention has demonstrated that QI is a cost-effective, evidence-based, and sustainable approach for strengthening integrated HIV, TB, malaria, RMNCH, community health, and emerging disease services without creating parallel systems. A phased expansion to additional states, supported by national leadership and alignment with Nigeria’s National Quality Policy and Strategy, would leverage existing learning, structures, and government capacity to accelerate institutionalization of QI nationally, reduce geographic inequities in quality of care and patient safety, improve efficiency through integrated service delivery, and support saturation of proven interventions across the health system.

Make QI a routine management function, not a project activity. The strongest sustainability lesson is that QI works best when it becomes part of how facilities, LGAs and states manage services every day. Facility QITs, SSMs, peer-learning platforms, dashboards and routine data reviews should be protected as continuous management tools, not treated as project-era activities. Protect state financing and budget execution. State ownership was demonstrated through operational plans, budget lines, government-led scale-up to 317 additional facilities and over ₦46 million mobilized through government structures. The next test is execution: ensuring that budgeted funds are released, tracked and used for supervision, mentorship, dashboards, community linkage and QI scale-up. Institutionalize QITs, SSMs and peer-learning platforms. The 865 Facility Quality Improvement Teams, 308 Supervisors Support Meetings and peer-learning platforms involving facility and community actors should remain embedded in routine state and LGA systems. These structures are the practical home of QI implementation and should not disappear after donor support ends. Embed QI coaching inside Integrated Supportive Supervision. QI coaching should remain part of routine Integrated Supportive Supervision. This is the most efficient way to avoid creating parallel supervision systems and to ensure that facility visits focus on problem-solving, mentoring and use of routine data. Use dashboards for management decisions, not only reporting. Government procurement of over 400 dashboards created a strong platform for perfo

Integrating QI into 15 health training institutions and training 127 lecturers and tutors created a long-term workforce pathway. This should be protected, monitored and expanded so that future healthcare workers enter the system with QI competencies before deployment. Keep community structures inside the improvement model. QI gains depended partly on community health workers, CHIPs, WDCs and community-facility linkages. These structures should remain part of the model because quality of care does not end at the facility door. Referrals, adherence, follow-up, demand generation and client experience all depend on strong community linkage. Scale carefully, using state readiness and facility performance data. The model is scalable, but expansion should be phased. Future scale-up should prioritize states and facilities where leadership, supervision systems, data-use routines, community structures and budget readiness are strong enough to sustain implementation. Link QI to Nigeria’s National Quality Policy and Strategy. The intervention can serve as a practical subnational implementation model for Nigeria’s National Quality Policy and Strategy. The next phase should use the learning from Ekiti, Gombe and Jigawa to strengthen national guidance, standardize approaches, and support wider institutionalization without creating a rigid one-size-fits-all model. Treat quality as preparedness infrastructure. Quality Improvement is not an optional service-delivery add-on. It is part of preparedness. This report presents an independent impact evaluation of the Global Fund COVID-19 Response Mechanism (C19RM) investments in Nigeria. The evaluation was commissioned in support of the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) and the National Tuberculosis and Leprosy Control Programme (NTBLCP) as Principal Recipients of the C19RM grant, and was conducted by Jhpiego Nigeria as the technical evaluation partner. The findings, interpretations, conclusions and recommendations expressed in this report are those of the evaluation team. They do not necessarily represent the official positions of the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, the Government of Nigeria, the Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare, NACA, NTBLCP, or any of the institutions that participated in the evaluation. The report draws on data collected from national, state and facility-level sources and on documentary records made available to the evaluation team. While reasonable measures were taken to verify and triangulate the information presented, the analysis reflects the data available at the time of fieldwork; investments that were ongoing at that time are identified as such, and conclusions concerning them are correspondingly qualified. All data are presented in de-identified form. Key informants are referred to by role and state, and no individual respondent can be identified from the contents of this report.

The COVID-19 pandemic was, among other things, a global stress test of the capacities that allow a health system to detect, report and respond to a public-health emergency. For Nigeria, the most populous country in Africa and a federation of thirty-six states and the Federal Capital Territory, that test arrived against a baseline the country had already chosen to measure. Nigeria conducted its first Joint External Evaluation (JEE) of International Health Regulations (IHR 2005) core capacities in June 2017, undertook a country-led midterm JEE in November 2019, and completed its second full JEE in August 2023. The first two assessments precede the pandemic; the third follows it. Together they give this evaluation something rare in health-systems work, namely an independent, externally validated measure of national capacity taken before and after the event whose response is being evaluated. *Figure 1. Nigeria conducted its first JEE in 2017 and its second in 2023, with a country-led midterm in 2019, giving a before-and-after measure of IHR core capacity around the pandemic and the C19RM investment.* The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria established the COVID-19 Response Mechanism (C19RM) in 2020 as its principal channel for supporting countries to respond to COVID-19. The mechanism was designed to do three things at once: - to provide an immediate response to the pandemic; - to protect the gains of programmes for HIV, tuberculosis and malaria (ATM) against pandemic-driven disruption; - and to make urgent improvements in the underlying systems for health.

In Nigeria, C19RM resources were realesed from 2021 to 2026, and the character of the investment changed materially across that window. What began as an acute commodity-and-response intervention, providing oxygen, diagnostics, personal protective equipment, case management and risk communication, matured into investments in laboratory networks, surveillance architecture, emergency operations, supply chains, the health workforce and digital health information that are properly understood as health-system strengthening for pandemic preparedness, and not as pandemic response alone. This evolution from response to resilience is the organising thesis of the evaluation. The analytical task is not only to ask what was delivered during the emergency, but whether the investment left behind durable, institutionalised capacity that measurably advances Nigeria's standing against the IHR core capacities and the JEE benchmarks. The pandemic-preparedness lens and the health-system-strengthening lens are, in this investment, two views of the same object, and the report treats them as such throughout.

The Nigeria C19RM portfolio was implemented through two Principal Recipients (PRs): the National Agency for the Control of AIDS (NACA) and the National Tuberculosis and Leprosy Control Programme (NTBLCP). Delivery was executed through Sub-Recipients drawn from government agencies, technical service organisations, technical-assistance partners and civil-society organisations. Implementation was carried out in concert with the federal institutions holding the relevant mandates, namely the Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare, the Nigeria Centre for Disease Control and Prevention (NCDC), the National Primary Health Care Development Agency (NPHCDA) and the national supply-chain and laboratory programmes, together with state ministries of health, state primary health care boards, community structures, the Country Coordinating Mechanism (CCM) and a range of development partners. Governance and oversight ran through the CCM and the Global Fund Country Team. This report follows the Global Fund modular framing. The C19RM investment is organised into thirteen **modules**, numbered M01 to M13, each mapping to one or more IHR core capacities and JEE technical areas. Several implementers, including the NCDC, refer to the same units as **pillars**; the two terms denote the same investment areas. The thirteen modules are set out below.

| Code | Module | Scope in brief | | --- | --- | --- | | **M01** | **Laboratory Systems Strengthening and Diagnostics** | Diagnostic capacity, laboratory equipment, sample-referral networks and quality systems for detecting COVID-19 and other priority pathogens. | | **M02** | **Surveillance System Strengthening** | Real-time, event-based and indicator-based disease surveillance, including the systems and data flows that enable early detection and reporting. | | **M03** | **Community Systems ****Strengthening and Risk Communication and Community Engagement (RCCE)** | Community structures, community-based surveillance, and the risk-communication and engagement capacity that sustains public trust and demand. | | **M04** | **Medical Oxygen, Respiratory Care and Therapeutics** | Oxygen production and distribution, respiratory-care equipment and therapeutics for the management of severe disease. | | **M05** | **Health Products, Supply Chain and Waste Management** | Warehousing, distribution and last-mile supply of health products, together with safe healthcare-waste management. | | **M06** | **Health Emergency Preparedness and Response (HEPR)** | Public-health emergency operations centres, incident-management systems, rapid-response teams and the emergency workforce. | | **M07** | **Human Resources for Health (HRH)** | The training, deployment and retention of the workforce that operates the capacities built across the portfolio. | | **M08** | **Infection Prevention and Control (IPC)** | IPC programmes, water and sanitation, the trained IPC workforce and IPC-enabling infrastructu

Effect on ATM Programmes** | Measures to protect the continuity of HIV, tuberculosis and malaria services against pandemic-driven disruption. | | **M11** | **COVID-19 Pandemic Response (Acute Phase)** | The immediate acute-phase response, including testing scale-up, case management, personal protective equipment and surge capacity. | | **M12** | **Programme Management and Coordination** | The two-Principal-Recipient architecture, coordination and grant-management arrangements through which the portfolio was delivered. | | **M13** | **Other Investment Areas: Quality Improvement** | Quality-improvement initiatives and reprogrammed activities not captured within the other twelve modules. |

The sequence moves from the diagnostic and detection capacities, through the response and system-support functions, to programme management and the cross-cutting quality lens. One boundary of scope should be stated at the outset. Health Financing and Financial Risk Protection, while central to the sustainability of every module, was not a C19RM investment line and is not evaluated as a module; the recurrent-financing question is instead examined within each module under the sustainability criterion, where it bites hardest.

t: 1.3 How to read this report: context and the maturity of impact
x: This report is written to be read in context, and two contexts in particular govern how its findings should be interpreted. The first is the maturity of the investments themselves. C19RM in Nigeria includes a substantial capital-investment component: warehouses, pressure-swing-adsorption oxygen plants, laboratory and facility upgrades, and revitalised primary health care centres. A portion of this infrastructure was not yet fully commissioned, handed over or in routine use at the time of fieldwork. Capital assets of this kind do not yield their health effects on the timescale of a grant; their value accrues over the working life of the asset. The evaluation therefore distinguishes, deliberately and throughout, between realised impact, which the evidence can demonstrate now, and prospective or contributory impact, which the investment is positioned to generate as the asset matures, is staffed and is maintained. Where an investment is not yet mature for impact, it is identified as such, and its plausible contribution is assessed.

t: 1.3 How to read this report: context and the maturity of impact
x: The second is the national strategy environment against which relevance and coherence are judged. Nigeria's health-security architecture rests on the IHR (2005) and the obligation to develop and maintain core capacities for prevention, detection and response, reported annually through the IHR States Parties Self-Assessment Annual Reporting (SPAR) instrument and assessed independently through the JEE. The 2017 JEE findings were operationalised in the National Action Plan for Health Security (NAPHS), Nigeria's costed roadmap for closing the capacity gaps the assessment identified, and the institutional anchor for much of this agenda is the NCDC, established in 2011 and given statutory backing by the NCDC Act of 2018. On the health-system side, the investment intersects with the National Health Act of 2014 and its Basic Health Care Provision Fund, the National Strategic Health Development Plan, the NPHCDA-led primary health care revitalisation agenda, and, most recently, the Nigeria Health Sector Renewal Investment Initiative (NHSRII) with its sector-wide approach organised around one plan, one budget and one monitoring framework. C19RM straddles these strategy generations, and the coherence analysis in each module asks specifically whether the investment advanced the relevant national plan rather than running alongside it. Globally, the investment sits within the Global Fund's framework for Resilient and Sustainable Systems for Health (RSSH) and the wider post-COVID reappraisal of health emergency preparedness, response and resilience. These frameworks are not background. The

t: 1.3 How to read this report: context and the maturity of impact
x: And C19RM is treated throughout as a **contributor**, not the sole cause of the changes observed, because it operated alongside government effort, concurrent Global Fund grants and other partners, often on the same platforms, and the report's language reflects this.

t: 1.4 Rationale for the evaluation: from pandemic response to health-system strengthening
x: The case for this evaluation rests on the trajectory of the investment itself. C19RM in Nigeria did not remain what it was at inception. It began as an emergency response that secured oxygen, tests, commodities and case-management capacity at the height of the pandemic. As the acute threat receded and reprogramming opportunities opened, it was directed increasingly toward the structural capacities that determine whether the system can withstand the next emergency: laboratory and surveillance networks, emergency operations centres and rapid-response teams, supply-chain and waste systems, the health workforce, infection prevention and control, and digital health information. This shift from response to resilience is visible in the programme record, and it is documented in detail in the End of Project Documentation (EOPD). The trajectory matters because the two phases make different promises and therefore demand different evidence. The response phase promised to save lives and maintain services under acute stress, and is judged largely on what it delivered at the time. The systems-strengthening phase promises a more prepared health system, and can be judged only by asking whether functional, used and durable capacity now exists, and whether Nigeria's measured position against the IHR core capacities and JEE benchmarks has moved. It is precisely because the investment evolved in this way that an impact evaluation is necessary. The value of the later, larger and more consequential investments cannot be read from delivery records alone. It can be read only from an assessment of c

The purpose of the evaluation is to provide a credible, independent, evidence-based assessment of Nigeria's C19RM grant implementation and of the impact of the subsequent systems-strengthening investments on the health system and on pandemic preparedness, identifying achievements, challenges and lessons that can inform future investment in resilient health systems and health security, including the design of Global Fund Grant Cycle 8. The specific objectives are the following. - To assess the **relevance** of C19RM investments to Nigeria's documented needs and to national and international frameworks, including the IHR core capacities, the JEE findings and the NAPHS. - To assess the **coherence** of the investments, both internally with national strategies and concurrent Global Fund grants and externally with the contributions of other partners. - To examine, through contribution analysis, the **effectiveness** of the investments in producing their intended intermediate outcomes, and the enablers and barriers that shaped delivery. - To assess the **efficiency** with which inputs were converted into outputs and outcomes, including the influence of the implementation architecture on timeliness and value. - To assess the **impact** of the investments on health-system performance and on Nigeria's pandemic-preparedness position, distinguishing realised from prospective and contributory impact, and measured in part against the movement in IHR and JEE capacity between 2017 and 2023. - To assess the **sustainability** and institutionalisation of the capacities created, including th
- To distil **lessons and strategic recommendations**, addressed to the institutions able to act on them.

The evaluation is structured around twenty-one evaluation questions (EQ1 to EQ21), organised by the six OECD-DAC criteria and the two cross-cutting lenses and common to all thirteen modules, so that findings can be compared across the portfolio and synthesised at the end. The module-specific phrasing of each question, with its denominators and indicators, is carried at the head of each chapter. Coherence is examined in two parts, internal and external. The set below is harmonised to the study protocol; verbatim wording should be read against protocol v1.4.

| Criterion | Evaluation questions | | --- | --- | | **Relevance** | EQ1 to EQ3: To what extent did the investment respond to documented needs; reach the tiers, geographies and populations where need was greatest; and adapt as the investment evolved from acute response toward systems strengthening? | | **Coherence** | EQ4 to EQ6: To what extent was the investment aligned with national strategies (NAPHS, the sector plans) and the IHR and JEE frameworks; coherent with concurrent Global Fund grants and government effort; and complementary to, rather than duplicative of, other partners? | | **Effectiveness** | EQ7 to EQ9: To what extent did the investment achieve its intended intermediate outcomes; what enablers and barriers shaped delivery; and what unintended outcomes emerged? | | **Efficiency** | EQ10 to EQ12: How efficiently were inputs converted into outputs in time, money and human resource; how did the implementation architecture affect efficiency; and where did delay, wastage or incomplete deployment occur? | | **Impact** | EQ13 to EQ15: What higher-order changes in health-system performance and preparedness can plausibly be linked to the investment; how has Nigeria's position against the IHR, JEE and NAPHS benchmarks moved between 2017 and 2023; and what impact is realised as against prospective or contributory? | | **Sustainability** | EQ16 to EQ18: Which capacities are likely to be sustained; through what financing, governance and human-resource mechanisms; and what threatens continuation once C19RM financing ends? | | **Equity** (cross-cutting) | EQ19 to EQ20: How e

and to what extent did it reach underserved and vulnerable populations, including persons with disabilities? | | **System risk** (cross-cutting) | EQ21: What residual risks to the health system remain, and which assumptions in the theory of change must hold for the gains to endure? |

The report is written first for the institutions that own and govern the investment and will act on its findings. The **Country Coordinating Mechanism (CCM)** will use it in its oversight and in shaping the next grant cycle. The two **Principal Recipients, NACA and NTBLCP**, will use it to account for implementation and to inform institutionalisation. The **government institutions that hold the pandemic-response and health-system mandates**, in particular the NCDC, the NPHCDA, the Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare, and the state governments, will use it to guide domestic financing, capacity retention and the next phase of health-security planning under the NAPHS and the NHSRII. Beyond these primary owners, the report serves the **Global Fund**, in-country and in Geneva, together with its Technical Review Panel and technical reference community, which will use it to understand what the investment achieved and to inform the design of Grant Cycle 8. It also serves **development partners** working on the same building blocks, and the **communities** the investment was intended to serve. Because these readers more often consult the chapter relevant to their mandate than read the report end to end, each module chapter is written to stand on its own, while the shared methodology that follows governs every chapter and is not repeated within them.

The evaluation is a theory-based, mixed-methods study using contribution analysis, executed at national scale. It drew on four triangulated evidence streams. It verified **1,599** C19RM investment sites across all **36 states and the Federal Capital Territory**, comprising **233** state-level fixed-asset census visits and **1,363** facility cascade-verification visits, conducted by **93** trained field personnel, of whom **76** administered the survey. It completed **270** key informant interviews, with the final number set by thematic saturation, and concentrated in-depth qualitative fieldwork in **15 priority states** across the six geopolitical zones. Impact is measured in part against the movement in IHR core capacity between the 2017 and 2023 Joint External Evaluations. *Figure 2. The evaluation in numbers.*

The evaluation is a theory-based, mixed-methods study built on contribution analysis. The design follows from the central inferential problem the evaluation must solve. C19RM was delivered into a complex, populous, federal system in which government, concurrent Global Fund grants and several development partners pursued overlapping objectives on shared platforms in the same period. Coverage of the relevant capacities was, by design, near-universal, and no untreated comparison group was held in reserve. A counterfactual design that seeks to attribute outcomes to C19RM alone is therefore not feasible, because the clean comparison it would require does not exist. The evaluation responds not with a single method but with a four-part identification strategy, in which each part compensates for the limits of the others. - **Contribution analysis against an explicit theory of change.** For each module the evaluation sets out how the investment was expected to work, tests that theory against the evidence, examines the alternative explanations, and reaches a transparent judgement on the strength of the contribution claim, rated against Mayne's six standards. - **A before-and-after anchor at the capacity level.** The 2017 JEE, with the 2019 midterm, and the 2023 JEE provide an independent, externally validated measure of IHR core-capacity scores before and after the pandemic. This is the closest available approximation to a pre-post design at the level the investment was meant to move, and it disciplines the impact narrative with an external metric rather than self-report alone.

- **Investment-level verification.** The IIRMS physically visited the investments to establish not merely that they were funded, but that they exist, function, are used, reach the intended populations and are positioned to endure. This verification-first logic grounds every higher-order claim in observed reality. - **Saturated qualitative causal testing.** Key informant interviews, conducted to thematic saturation across the implementation hierarchy, supply the causal interpretation, that is, how and why change did or did not occur, which the quantitative streams cannot provide on their own. *Figure 3. The four-part identification strategy; each line of evidence offsets the limits of the others.* Together, these four lines of evidence allow the evaluation to triangulate a defensible account of C19RM's contribution, bounded by what the evidence supports.

Four frameworks are layered so that each does distinct analytical work. The **WHO health-system building blocks** provide the structural map and locate each module within the system, so that the investment is read as a set of changes to a system rather than a list of procurements. The **IHR (2005) core capacities, the JEE benchmarks and the NAPHS** provide the health-security yardstick: each module is mapped to the IHR core capacity and JEE technical area it is intended to advance, and the impact analysis asks how the investment moved, or is positioned to move, Nigeria's standing against those benchmarks, measured concretely against the 2017-to-2023 JEE trajectory. The **Global Fund RSSH framework** supplies the programme theory linking disease-programme investment to system-wide resilience, and is the basis on which coherence is judged. The **OECD-DAC criteria** of relevance, coherence, effectiveness, efficiency, impact and sustainability provide the normative basis for evaluative judgement and structure the findings in every module. Two cross-cutting lenses are applied across all six criteria rather than treated separately. **Equity** is examined through a PROGRESS-Plus logic spanning geography, facility tier, the public and private sectors, gender and vulnerable populations including persons with disabilities, supported where data permit by concentration indices and rate ratios. **System risk** is examined through a resilience lens that asks what residual risk remains and which assumptions must hold for the gains to endure.

A portfolio-level theory of change articulates the causal logic the evaluation tests. It follows the standard results chain, in which inputs enable activities, activities produce outputs, outputs generate intermediate outcomes, and intermediate outcomes contribute to longer-term outcomes and impact on health-system performance and preparedness. It makes explicit the two bands of conditions on which the chain depends. The upper band comprises the assumptions that must hold for each stage to lead to the next, for instance that equipment delivered is installed, staffed and maintained. The lower band comprises the external and contextual factors the investment does not control, such as concurrent partner investment, security conditions, and the release of domestic budget. Each module carries its own theory of change in the same form, and the contribution analysis proceeds by testing its links and assumptions against the evidence.

t: 2.4 Contribution analysis and the strength-of-evidence rating
x: For each module and each OECD-DAC criterion, the strength of the contribution claim is assessed against Mayne's six standards for a credible contribution story: a reasoned theory of change; activities implemented as planned; a theory of change verified by the evidence; other influencing factors assessed; the main alternative explanations examined and either ruled out or accommodated; and an account validated by stakeholders. Each module presents this assessment in a standard six-row table and states an overall contribution claim that is explicit about its limits. The evidence supporting each criterion is summarised in a four-point strength-of-evidence rating, namely strong, moderate, limited, or insufficient, reflecting the convergence of the evidence streams, the quality of the underlying data, and the extent to which alternative explanations were tested. The rating describes the strength of the evidence for the contribution claim. It is not a score awarded to the investment. The precise thresholds of the scale are settled at validation so that they are applied uniformly across all thirteen modules.

The evaluation triangulates four streams, and a finding gains weight to the extent that they converge. The **Investment Impact and Reach Mapping Survey (IIRMS)** is the quantitative backbone, described in Section 2.6. The **key informant interviews (KIIs)** supply the causal interpretation, using a dual tool architecture of module guides and cross-cutting role-based guides anchored to the OECD-DAC criteria. **Documentary and administrative data**, comprising programme and procurement records, assessment reports, and the national strategy and health-security documents including the JEE and the NAPHS, establish what was planned and reported and corroborate the other streams. **Direct observation with before-and-after photographic verification** confirms the physical existence and condition of assets during site visits.

t: 2.6 The Investment Impact and Reach Mapping Survey and the sample
x: The IIRMS is an **investment-level survey**. Its unit of observation is the C19RM investment itself, and its purpose is to visit that investment and verify, on site, not only whether it exists and functions but whether it is utilised, whom it reaches, what impact it is generating and whether it is positioned to be sustained. The sample frame was built bottom-up from the documented C19RM investment universe, using the State Mapping together with the oxygen, surveillance, laboratory, IPC, case-management and mitigation locations matrices, reconciled against the Performance Framework. The instrument's modular architecture captures every investment co-located at a single site within one visit, without double-counting. Where the universe of investments in a module is small, the IIRMS visits every site, which is a census. Where it is large, the IIRMS visits a stratified sample with a fixed allocation per state, so that every state and the FCT is represented in the equity analysis.

t: 2.6 The Investment Impact and Reach Mapping Survey and the sample
x: The realised sample is as follows. The IIRMS was administered nationally across all thirty-six states and the Federal Capital Territory, with **1,599 completed site visits**, comprising **233 state-level fixed-asset census visits** and **1,363 facility cascade-verification visits**; respondent consent was obtained at 1,596 of the 1,599 visits. Fieldwork was conducted by **93 trained field personnel** organised into six geopolitical clusters under cluster leads and a national field coordinator, of whom **76 administered the IIRMS**, at an average of approximately 24 visits per enumerator across the field window. In-depth qualitative fieldwork was concentrated in **fifteen priority states**, two to three per geopolitical zone, selected to represent the country's epidemiological and developmental diversity from high-capacity southern states to fragile, security-affected northern ones. A total of **270 key informant interviews** were completed, against a planning range of 245 to 285, with the final number determined by **saturation tracking** rather than a fixed quota. The frame was derived bottom-up by enumerating the senior decision-makers, technical leads and frontline implementers whose perspectives the contribution analysis requires, each counted once. Because many senior actors in Nigeria's health system credibly span several modules, they were interviewed once, using role-based tools covering the modules within their authority, and counted once. The design is grounded in established saturation principles for thematic analysis, under which nine to seventeen interviews typ

Three families of tools were deployed: the IIRMS; the KII guides, in two complementary families comprising module guides aligned to each module and cross-cutting role-based guides for senior respondents whose authority spans modules; and administrative-data templates for each module. All are grounded in the C19RM Theory of Change, the Global Fund C19RM Performance Framework and Modular Framework, and the validated indicator sets established by the technical team. Quantitative data were collected through computer-assisted personal interviewing with built-in consistency checks and photographic capture at the point of observation. Fieldwork was preceded by structured enumerator and interviewer training and was supervised in the field to maintain consistency across the team and the thirty-seven jurisdictions.

Quantitative IIRMS data were cleaned, validated and analysed descriptively, using counts and percentages for categorical variables and means, medians and inter-quartile ranges for continuous ones, with disaggregation by geography, facility tier and sector to support the equity analysis. Comparisons across states used chi-squared and Fisher's exact tests for categorical variables and rank-based tests for skewed continuous variables, with false-discovery-rate adjustment where multiple comparisons were made within a module. For impact, the analysis combined trend analysis of the C19RM Performance Framework and of IHR, JEE and NAPHS indicators across 2020 to 2026 with before-and-after comparison anchored to a 2020 or 2021 baseline, and with interrupted time-series or difference-in-differences techniques where the data permitted. Qualitative data were analysed thematically through a hybrid deductive-inductive approach, with deductive codes derived from the OECD-DAC criteria and the module theories of change and inductive codes allowed to emerge, supported by inter-coder checks. Responses about activities still ongoing at fieldwork were flagged in the analysis dataset to avoid claiming closure on incomplete investments.

For each evaluation question, the evidence was assembled in a structured triangulation matrix that compared the primary and corroborating streams and recorded their convergence or divergence, with each criterion tied to the Mayne standard it principally serves. The logic is summarised below, and the full matrix is provided in the methodological annex. | Criterion | Primary evidence | Corroboration | Principal Mayne standard | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Relevance | KIIs at policy and PR/SR level; document review (NAPHS, NHSRII, JEE) | IIRMS contextual fields; partner mapping | Standard 1 (reasoned theory of change) | | Coherence | Document review; KIIs across actors | Partner mapping; concurrent-grant records | Standards 4 and 5 (other factors, alternatives) | | Effectiveness | IIRMS output and functionality data; facility and state KIIs | Photographic verification; training and administrative records | Standards 2 and 3 (implementation, results chain) | | Efficiency | Administrative and procurement data; KIIs on process | IIRMS timeliness and deployment fields | Standard 2 (implementation as planned) | | Impact | Trend analysis of PF and IHR/JEE/NAPHS indicators 2020 to 2026; before-and-after; IIRMS reach | KIIs to interpret change; equity analyses | Standards 3, 4 and 5 | | Sustainability | KIIs on financing and governance; budget and policy documents | IIRMS maintenance and funding-source fields | Standards 4 and 6 (other factors, validation) | Module scorecards were then synthesised into the portfolio-level performance dashboard and the IHR, JEE and NAPHS capacity-movem

Quality was assured at every stage. Instruments were piloted and refined before deployment. Field personnel were trained against a common protocol and supervised by cluster leads, a national field coordinator and a dedicated quality-assurance lead. Quantitative data were validated and consistency-checked, with daily upload from the field. Qualitative coding was subject to inter-coder reliability checks. Findings were triangulated across streams before they were reported, so that no single-source claim carried decisive weight. Provisional findings and ratings were prepared for stakeholder validation, and the contribution claims are stated as provisional pending that validation and the finalisation of the IIRMS dataset.

The evaluation was reviewed and approved by two ethics bodies before any data were collected: the institutional review board (IRB) of the implementing partner, and the National Health Research Ethics Committee (NHREC) of the Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare. Any amendment after ethics approval required re-review. | **IRB approval number** | [ insert IRB protocol or approval number ] | | --- | --- | | **NHREC approval number** | [ insert NHREC approval number and date ] | Informed consent was obtained from all participants. Participation was voluntary. Confidentiality and anonymity were maintained, with key informants identified by role and state and never by name. Data-protection and safeguarding obligations were observed throughout, including a designated safeguarding focal person on the field team. The report presents de-identified data, and no individual respondent can be identified from its contents.

Four limitations bear on interpretation, and each was mitigated as far as the design allowed. First, and most consequentially, the near-universal coverage of C19RM and the presence of concurrent government and partner investment mean there is no clean counterfactual. This is mitigated by the four-part identification strategy of Section 2.1, namely contribution analysis, the 2017-to-2023 JEE pre-post anchor, investment-level verification and saturated qualitative testing, and by the consistent use of contributor rather than attributive language. Second, a material share of the capital portfolio was not yet mature for impact at the time of fieldwork. Such investments are identified as such and analysed under prospective or contributory impact rather than counted as realised. Third, administrative and programme records were of variable completeness, and were corroborated against survey, interview and observational evidence rather than relied upon alone. Fourth, the boundary between C19RM and concurrent partner contributions was, in some accounts, difficult to draw on shared platforms; where this occurs it is noted, and the contribution is assessed as plausible rather than exclusive. Each of these shapes the confidence attached to particular claims, and the strength-of-evidence ratings reflect them accordingly. Nigeria C19RM Impact Evaluation · Impact Evaluation Report Page 1

C19RM was delivered by three Principal Recipients kept analytically distinct and never summed: NACA (lead/financial), NTBLCP (disease programme), and Lagos State Ministry of Health (sub-national). NCDC held the statutory pandemic-response mandate under the NCDC Act 2018 but operated as a zero-cash sub-recipient — the mandate-money split. Inviolable denominators: 22 state warehouses, 236 Tier-1 IIRMS sites, 95 PSA oxygen plants, 157 PHCs, 37 states and the FCT.

Medical oxygen (Module 02). C19RM invested in 95 PSA oxygen plants in total, of which 73 were newly procured and 22 were repaired or upgraded and handed over. It also supported 12 liquid-oxygen (LOX) tanks, together with oxygen concentrators, flow meters, humidifiers and medical cylinders. Delivered through the national Principal Recipients. Headline answer: 95 oxygen plants (73 newly procured, 22 repaired).

t: Supply chain and health products investment headline
x: Supply chain, storage and waste (Module 08). C19RM invested in 22 state warehouses across 21 states, 1,548 motorbikes for last-mile distribution, about 2,086 uninterruptible power supply (UPS) units, and solar power equipment (batteries, inverters, charge controllers and racks) to keep facilities and cold chain running. Headline supply-chain list: 22 warehouses in 21 states; 1,548 motorbikes; 2,086 UPS units; solar power and battery systems.

C19RM verified headline counts. 22 state warehouses across 21 states. 95 PSA oxygen plants, of which 73 newly procured and 22 repaired. 12 liquid-oxygen tanks. 157 primary health centres (PHCs) revitalised. 1,548 motorbikes. 236 Tier-1 IIRMS survey sites. Coverage across 37 states and the FCT. The three Principal Recipients (NACA, NTBLCP, Lagos State Ministry of Health) are reported separately and never summed.
